plants.nativeContext: Native to the rainforest understorey of Peru and neighboring South America, carpeting damp, shaded forest floors.
plants.notes: The Nerve Plant is a great starting point for closed builds. It wilts dramatically when dry but bounces back quickly with water, so don't panic when it droops. Keep out of direct light.

plants.nativeContext: A spikemoss from the humid subtropical forests of southern China.
plants.notes: Rainbow Moss has iridescent blue-green foliage that needs consistently high humidity to stay vibrant. It thrives in sealed containers and spreads as a low ground cover.
Java moss is a very forgiving plant. It can be fixed to wood or rocks, or left to spread as ground cover — it adapts well either way and handles a wide temperature range without complaint.
plants.nativeContext: From fast-flowing streams and riverbanks of Southeast Asia, anchored to submerged rocks and wood.
Native to shaded, damp rocks on islands of the western Mediterranean.
plants.notes: Baby Tears is a tiny-leaved creeping ground cover that can spread aggressively, so trim it regularly. It prefers cooler temperatures than most tropical species.
Trailing Watermelon Begonia has beautiful silver-green patterned leaves and trails nicely over substrate or rocks. Despite the common name, it’s not a true begonia nor does it make watermelons.
plants.nativeContext: Native to the shaded, humid forests of Vietnam and neighboring Southeast Asia, trailing across damp forest floors.
Native to the humid tropical rainforests of Brazil, on the shaded forest floor.
plants.notes: The leaves on the Prayer Plant fold up at night. In the wild, this is to preserve moisture and avoid damage. It’s very sensitive to minerals like fluoride in its water. Prone to [[spider-mites|spider mites]], especially if conditions run dry.

plants.nativeContext: From the warm, humid rainforests of Colombia and Ecuador, in deep shade beneath the canopy.
plants.notes: The Pinstripe Calathea has striking pink-striped leaves that are sensitive to drafts, cold, and mineral-heavy water. It does best in a stable sealed environment where conditions don’t fluctuate.
Polka Dot Plant is a fast grower that needs regular tip [[pinching]] to stay compact. Growth becomes leggy and color fades without adequate indirect light.
plants.nativeContext: Native to the humid forests of Madagascar, its vivid leaf patterning standing out in the shaded understorey.
From humid, shaded ravines and waterfall splash zones in tropical South America.
plants.notes: The Maidenhair Fern has fan-shaped [[frond|fronds]] that are beautiful but unforgiving. Changes in humidity can cause frond drop with little warning. It’s one of the more demanding ferns, but it genuinely thrives in a closed build where conditions stay stable.

plants.nativeContext: An epiphytic fern of tropical African, Asian ,and Pacific rainforests, perched in the forks of canopy trees.
plants.notes: Bird’s Nest Fern has bold, glossy strap-shaped [[frond|fronds]] and is more tolerant than most delicate ferns. Avoid letting water sit in the crown. It can get very large, so it’s best suited to bigger builds.
Friendship Plant has deeply textured, bronze-green leaves and is easy to propagate from [[node]] cuttings. It lives happily in closed builds.
plants.nativeContext: Native to the tropical rainforests of Central and South America, creeping across damp, shaded forest floors.
An aquatic fern from the streams and riverbanks of Southeast Asia, attached to rocks and wood.
plants.notes: Java Fern’s [[rhizome]] needs to be attached to wood or rock rather than buried in substrate. It propagates via [[plantlet|plantlets]] that form on older [[frond|fronds]].
Flame Violet has metallic copper-green foliage with orange-red flowers. It needs warmth and cold drafts can cause leaves to drop. It spreads via [[stolon|stolons]].
plants.nativeContext: From the shaded, humid rainforest floors of Colombia and Central America, forming creeping mats of velvety patterned leaves.

plants.toxicity: Contains insoluble calcium oxalate crystals — chewing or ingesting causes intense mouth and throat irritation, drooling, and vomiting in pets and people. Keep away from curious pets and children.
plants.notes: Heartleaf Philodendron has a trailing or climbing habit that needs regular trimming to stay at a manageable scale in enclosed builds. It’s very adaptable and forgiving overall.
plants.nativeContext: Native to the humid forests of Central America and the Caribbean, climbing trees and trailing across the shaded forest floor.

plants.notes: Miniature Orchid is a pleurothallid (A large subset of the orchid family) type that prefers good airflow even in high humidity. It can be mounted on cork bark or fine sphagnum rather than planted in substrate.
plants.nativeContext: From the cool, misty cloud forests of the Colombian and Ecuadorean Andes, growing as a tiny epiphyte on mossy tree bark.

plants.notes: Emerald Ripple Peperomia has textured, rippled leaves and semi-succulent stems that store some water. Prefers the top of the substrate to dry slightly between waterings.
plants.nativeContext: From the humid tropical forests of Brazil, growing as a compact groundcover in deep shade on the forest floor.
From the humid forests of tropical Central and South America, trailing over mossy rocks and tree branches in dappled light.
plants.notes: Trailing Jade has delicate stems with small round leaves that cascade nicely over edges of backgrounds or wood.

plants.nativeContext: A terrestrial bromeliad from the shaded forest floors of eastern Brazil.
plants.notes: Earth Star Bromeliad is a star-shaped rosette that holds water in its central cup. [[pup|Pups]] freely after flowering.
Dwarf Mondo Grass forms compact, grass-like tufts and is very cold-tolerant, making it one of the few viable options for cooler builds. It works well as a ground-cover accent.
plants.nativeContext: From the forest floors of China, Japan, and Vietnam, forming low grass-like mats in shaded, moist conditions.

plants.toxicity: Foliage and sap carry calcium oxalate crystals; ingestion irritates the mouth and digestive tract and the sap can irritate skin. Toxic to cats and dogs. See more information on the NCSU page.
plants.nativeContext: From the humid tropical forests of Latin America, climbing and trailing in dappled light beneath the canopy.
plants.notes: Arrowhead Plant has compact juvenile growth with arrow-shaped leaves, but mature climbing growth isn’t suitable for enclosed builds, so trim it frequently.
Creeping Charlie is a fast-spreading carpet plant with tiny round leaves. It’s easy to control with trimming.
plants.nativeContext: From the humid lowland forests of Central and South America, carpeting the shaded forest floor.

plants.toxicity: Begonias contain soluble calcium oxalates, most concentrated in the tubers; ingestion can cause mouth irritation, drooling, and vomiting in cats and dogs.
plants.notes: Rex Begonia is grown for its metallic, patterned foliage. Without enough bright indirect light, it can lose the metallic look. High humidity is essential, but water sitting on the leaves causes [[botrytis]], so keep the leaves dry.
plants.nativeContext: Originally from the humid forests of Assam, India; the modern Rex Begonia is a complex hybrid group grown for its spectacular patterned foliage.
From shaded, rocky ground in the Eastern Cape of South Africa, often sheltered under shrubs.
plants.notes: Zebra Haworthia prefers bright indirect light and tolerates low light better than most succulents, but direct sun can scorch the leaves. It has distinctive white warty stripes on its leaves.

plants.nativeContext: From semi-arid rocky outcrops in the highlands of Mexico, in bright sun and fast-draining ground.
plants.notes: Mexican Snowball is a classic rosette succulent. It needs bright direct sun to maintain its compact form, and will grow leggy without enough light. It [[pup|pups]] prolifically.
Jelly Bean Plant has bean-shaped leaves that turn red at the tips with sun stress. The stems are brittle, so handle it carefully. Leaves propagate easily.
plants.nativeContext: Native to rocky hillsides in Mexico, where its plump, colored leaves store water through dry seasons.

plants.toxicity: Jade is toxic to cats and dogs; ingestion can cause vomiting, lethargy, and incoordination. Keep away from pets.
plants.notes: Mini Jade Plant is a dwarf jade that stays compact for years. Water only when completely dry. It’s a good choice for rock-garden style open terrariums.
plants.nativeContext: From the rocky hillsides and dry scrublands of the Eastern Cape and KwaZulu-Natal, South Africa.
Native to the forested foothills of the Himalayas in Yunnan, China.
plants.notes: Chinese Money Plant has distinctive round, pancake-shaped leaves and prefers cooler temperatures than most tropical species. Rotate it regularly for even growth, and expect it to [[pup]] freely at the base.

plants.toxicity: The sap causes contact dermatitis in pets and can irritate sensitive skin in people; ingestion may cause mild digestive upset. Listed toxic to cats and dogs.
plants.nativeContext: Native to the subtropical forests of southeastern South America, carpeting the ground in damp, shaded conditions.
plants.notes: Wandering Dude is a very fast-growing trailing plant that can overwhelm smaller companions, so trim it frequently. (You know the type.) Purple-leaf varieties need brighter light to retain their color.

plants.nativeContext: Found throughout temperate forests of the Northern Hemisphere, forming dense cushions on acidic, shaded soil and tree bases.
plants.notes: Cushion Moss forms dense, velvety light-green clumps and works well as a primary ground cover in micro containers and jars. Like charcoal, it can absorb minerals from water.
Pocket Moss is a feathery aquatic moss that grows well on damp wood or rocks. It’s a good choice for miniature sealed setups.
plants.nativeContext: A submerged aquatic moss from the clear, cool streams of temperate and subtropical regions worldwide.

plants.notes: Rabbit’s Foot Fern has furry surface [[rhizome|rhizomes]] that resemble animal feet -- It’s important not to trim these! It thrives in both closed and well-ventilated open terrariums.
plants.nativeContext: From the humid tropical forests of Fiji and surrounding Pacific islands, its furry rhizomes creeping over rocks and tree bark.

plants.notes: Mother Spleenwort is a graceful fern that grows miniature plantlets directly on its [[frond|fronds]]. It prefers stable, highly humid closed systems.
plants.nativeContext: From the cool, moist forest gullies and shaded cliff faces of New Zealand and southeastern Australia.

plants.notes: Watermelon Peperomia has leaves patterned like watermelon rinds. Its semi-succulent stems make it sensitive to overwatering in closed glass.
plants.nativeContext: From the humid rainforest floors of Bolivia and western South America, growing in deep shade beneath the forest canopy.
Native to the tropical forests of Central America and the Caribbean, growing in shaded, moist understorey.
plants.notes: Baby Rubber Plant has thick, glossy leaves that store moisture and prefers some airflow. It’s a durable choice for larger open terrariums, and closed ones should be ventilated regularly.
Mini Bucephalandra is an elegant, slow-growing plant with dark metallic hues. It needs to be attached to rock or wood rather than buried, and is well suited to micro sealed jars.
plants.nativeContext: Endemic to the fast-moving streams and rivers of Sarawak, Borneo, anchored to rocks in clear, cool, shaded water.
A slow-growing aquatic aroid from the shaded streams and rivers of West and Central Africa, anchored to rocks and submerged wood.
plants.notes: Anubias Petite is an extremely robust miniature aquatic plant that thrives in waterlogged, hyper-humid conditions, especially on wood or hardscape inside tiny jars. Covering the [[rhizome]] will cause rot.
Dwarf Pennywort produces small clover-like leaves on fast-growing creeping [[stolon|runners]]. It forms a dense green carpet if trimmed regularly.
plants.nativeContext: A creeping aquatic plant from the shallow streams and wetland margins of Southeast Asia and northern Australia.

plants.notes: Silver Sparkle Pilea is a trailing plant with red stems and tiny metallic blue-silver leaves. It spreads quickly and looks best cascading downward.
plants.nativeContext: From the shaded tropical forests of Central America, spreading as a low, delicate groundcover in moist conditions.

plants.toxicity: Pothos contains insoluble calcium oxalates; ingestion causes oral irritation, drooling, and vomiting. Toxic to cats and dogs.
plants.notes: Miniature Pothos is a scaled-down cultivar of the standard pothos vine. Highly adaptable, it handles trailing or climbing equally well.
plants.nativeContext: Native to the humid tropical forests of Southeast Asia, climbing tree trunks and spreading across the forest floor.

plants.notes: Creeping Primrose-Willow is an amphibious species whose leaves develop attractive reddish tones under bright light. It does particularly well in swampy closed layouts.
plants.nativeContext: A semi-aquatic plant native to the margins of streams and ponds from the southern USA to Argentina.

plants.notes: This Java Moss grows in triangular, fern-like branch structures resembling a pine tree. It’s highly forgiving and well suited to miniature setups.
plants.nativeContext: From streams and wetlands of Java and Singapore, growing submerged or emergent on rocks and wood in gentle current.

plants.notes: Miniature Baby Tears is a very sad name (Who is making miniature babies cry?) and it is even smaller and more compact than standard Baby Tears. It forms an ultra-dense, bright green carpet.
plants.nativeContext: A miniature cultivar of a Mediterranean island endemic, native to shaded, damp rocky slopes of Corsica, Sardinia, and nearby islands.
An epiphytic jungle cactus of tropical American rainforests, dangling from tree branches.
plants.notes: Mistletoe Cactus is an epiphytic jungle cactus that prefers indirect light rather than direct sun. It tolerates well-monitored closed terrariums better than most cacti with occasional ventilation and good drainage.

plants.nativeContext: From the winter-rainfall fynbos of South Africa's Western Cape, in permanently damp, nutrient-poor seeps.
plants.notes: Cape Sundew is a forgiving beginner [[carnivore]], but it must only receive rain or distilled water as minerals are lethal to it. It wants bright light and airflow, as the sticky dew drops in stale air.

plants.nativeContext: A highland pitcher plant endemic to the Philippines, on mossy mountain slopes in cool, humid cloud forest.
plants.notes: Tropical Pitcher Plant is a tolerant highland Nepenthes that pitchers well in humid enclosures. It should have an airy, chunky mix and should never stay soggy. Minerals can be harmful, so use distilled water only.

plants.nativeContext: Native to rocky, seasonally dry slopes in the mountains of Mexico and Central America.
plants.notes: Mexican Butterwort uses its sticky leaves to catch [[fungus-gnats|gnats]] and prefers a mineral, near-neutral mix, which is unusual for a [[carnivore]]. It has a drier winter succulent phase, so ease off watering during that time.
Lemon Button Fern has compact, lemon-scented button [[frond|fronds]] and is one of the easiest ferns for both open and closed setups. Keep it evenly moist.
plants.nativeContext: From the humid tropical forests of Asia and the Pacific, growing as a compact fern in shaded, moist conditions.

plants.notes: Silver Lace Fern is a variegated cretan brake fern. Its airy [[frond|fronds]] suit medium-sized planted terraria.
plants.nativeContext: Widely distributed from the Mediterranean through Asia and Africa, growing in shaded rocky slopes, walls, and forest edges.

plants.notes: Crocodile Fern has distinctive puckered, crocodile-skin [[frond|fronds]]. It’s an epiphyte at heart and loves humidity, but the roots should never sit in waterlogged substrate.
plants.nativeContext: From the humid tropical forests of Southeast Asia and northern Australia, its distinctive scaly fronds emerging as a large epiphyte on tree trunks.

plants.notes: Golden Clubmoss is a carpeting spikemoss that needs constant humidity and crisps quickly in open air. It works well as ground cover in sealed jars.
plants.nativeContext: From the moist, shaded forests and stream margins of tropical and southern Africa, carpeting the ground in humid conditions.

plants.notes: Sheet Moss is a flat carpeting moss that knits into a lawn over substrate and hardscape. Mist to establish it, and it thrives on the high humidity of a closed lid.
plants.nativeContext: A widespread temperate forest moss, forming flat green sheets over soil, rocks, and fallen wood in moist, shaded woodlands.

plants.notes: Mood Moss forms plush cushions that flex visibly with humidity changes. It wants shade and a sealed, humid microclimate, and dislikes being buried or sitting in standing water.
plants.nativeContext: Found throughout cool temperate forests of the Northern Hemisphere, forming arching cushions on acidic, shaded soil.

plants.toxicity: An aroid containing calcium oxalate crystals; ingestion irritates the mouth and digestive tract. Toxic to cats and dogs.
plants.nativeContext: From the rainforests of southern Thailand and Malaysia, climbing tree trunks toward the light.
plants.notes: Mini Monstera is a fast-growing fenestrated climber that needs climbing support in larger open terraria to avoid sprawling. It’s too vigorous for small sealed jars.

plants.toxicity: An aroid containing calcium oxalate crystals; sap can irritate skin and ingestion irritates the mouth and gut. Toxic to cats and dogs.
plants.nativeContext: A small epiphytic aroid of Central and South American rainforests, perched on branches and trunks.
plants.notes: Anthurium scandens is a small epiphytic anthurium that produces pearly berries. It needs an airy, bark-rich mix and high humidity, and does well climbing a background in a closed tank. It reaches around 60 cm without a support and up to 180 cm with something to climb.

plants.toxicity: Peace lily contains insoluble calcium oxalate crystals; ingestion irritates the mouth and throat and causes drooling and vomiting. Toxic to cats and dogs.
plants.nativeContext: Native to the humid rainforest floor of Central and South America.
plants.notes: Peace Lily is low-light tolerant and droops visibly when conditions run dry. A compact specimen suits medium terraria but may need to be removed once it outgrows the space.

plants.toxicity: Begonias contain soluble calcium oxalates, most concentrated in the tubers; ingestion can cause mouth irritation, drooling, and vomiting in cats and dogs.
plants.nativeContext: From the dim understorey of Malaysian montane rainforest, where its leaves shimmer blue in low light.
plants.notes: Peacock Begonia has iridescent blue leaves caused by structural color that only develops in low light. It needs sealed humidity and shade to maintain the shimmer.

plants.toxicity: Begonias contain soluble calcium oxalates, most concentrated in the tubers; ingestion can cause mouth irritation, drooling, and vomiting in cats and dogs.
plants.notes: Polka Dot Begonia has silver-spotted wing-like leaves that prefer airflow over a fully sealed lid since it’s prone to mildew.
plants.nativeContext: Native to the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil, growing in the humid, shaded understorey.

plants.notes: Lipstick Plant is an epiphytic trailer with tubular red blooms. It works best in an airy epiphyte mix.
plants.nativeContext: An epiphytic gesneriad from the humid rainforests of Southeast Asia, perched on tree branches in dappled canopy light.

plants.notes: Cape Primrose is a long-blooming gesneriad suited to open, airy terraria. Water the substrate rather than the crown, and let the top dry slightly between waterings.
plants.nativeContext: From the moist, shaded cliffs and forest margins of southern Africa's Cape region.

plants.toxicity: String of pearls is toxic if ingested — causing drooling, vomiting, and lethargy in pets — and the sap can irritate skin. Mildly toxic to people.
plants.nativeContext: Native to the dry scrublands of south-western Africa, trailing over rock in the shade of larger plants.
plants.notes: String of Pearls is a bead-like trailing succulent for the edge of an open dish. It cannot handle humidity or [[wet-feet|wet feet]], so keep it in an open container with gritty substrate that dries between soaks.

plants.nativeContext: From the arid gravel plains of southern Africa, mimicking surrounding stones to escape grazing.
plants.notes: Living Stones are mimicry succulents shaped and patterned to look like the pebbles they grow among. (They have committed to this bit for roughly 37 million years.) They need direct sun, a mineral alkaline grit mix, and essentially no water through winter. Never grow them in a closed container; any trapped humidity will rot them.

plants.nativeContext: Native to arid limestone hills in central Mexico.
plants.notes: Thimble Cactus is a clustering cactus that throws [[offset|offsets]] constantly — they detach at the slightest touch and root easily wherever they land. (It is, reproductively speaking, extremely optimistic.) It needs bright direct sun and a sharp, [[alkaline]]-leaning grit mix. Never grow it in a closed container; trapped humidity will cause rot.

plants.nativeContext: From shaded cliffs in southern Mexico, where its stems trail down rock faces.
plants.notes: Burro’s Tail trails long, dense ropes of plump blue-green leaves that detach at the slightest touch. If you brush the stems while positioning it, you’ll lose a handful of leaves without trying. It needs an open container, a fast-draining dry substrate, and plenty of light.

plants.toxicity: Ficus sap (a milky latex) can irritate skin and the mouth; ingestion may cause mild digestive upset. Toxic to cats and dogs.
plants.nativeContext: From the forests and rocky slopes of East Asia, climbing tree trunks and walls.
plants.notes: Creeping Fig is a tiny-leaved, self-clinging vine that carpets any surface it contacts — glass, cork, stone, substrate, other plants. In a closed tank, it will blanket everything in a matter of months without regular [[pinching]] or trimming. It’s one of the easiest backgrounds to establish, but plan to manage it actively.

Strawberry Begonia is neither strawberry nor begonia (an identity crisis decades in the making), but a hardy [[saxifrage]] rosette that spreads by throwing small plantlets on long runners. It prefers cooler temperatures and tolerates both open and lidded setups, which makes it unusually flexible among terrarium plants.
plants.nativeContext: From the cool, shaded forest floors of China and Japan, sending out long [[stolon|runners]] across mossy ground.
Native to the southern Chinese provinces of Guizhou and Yunnan, as well as Vietnam.
plants.notes: Aluminum Plant has silver-splashed, quilted leaves and grows quickly into a full, bushy shape. Regular [[pinching]] keeps it dense rather than leggy. It does better with airflow than under a sealed lid, so a vented enclosure or open container suits it better than a fully closed build.

plants.toxicity: The sap causes contact dermatitis in pets and can irritate sensitive skin in people; ingestion may cause mild digestive upset. Listed toxic to cats and dogs.
plants.nativeContext: Native to the humid tropical forests of Mexico and Central America, trailing across the shaded forest floor.
plants.notes: Inch Plant is a fast-growing purple-striped trailer that can provide ground coverage in open terrariums within weeks. It gets leggy fast — bare stems with a tuft of leaves at the tips — and benefits from being cut back hard when that happens. Cuttings will propagate easily!

plants.nativeContext: From the deeply shaded, mossy rainforest floors of Borneo, Malaysia, and the Philippines, growing in moist leaf litter under dense canopy.
plants.notes: Lightning Bolt Orchid is one of the most striking jewel orchids, grown for its dark, velvety leaves threaded with electric-gold [[venation]] that shimmers under light. It should be planted shallowly in damp sphagnum with good drainage below — soggy roots will kill it, and so will any direct light.

plants.nativeContext: From the dark, humid forest floors of Southeast Asia — Myanmar, southern China, and Indonesia — commonly found on the forest floor.
plants.notes: Black Jewel Orchid is the most forgiving jewel orchid — its deep maroon-black leaves have parallel red-gold pin-stripe [[venation]], and its creeping stems root readily at every [[node]]. Shallow planting works best, letting the stems sprawl along the substrate surface rather than burying them.

plants.nativeContext: Native to the tropical rainforests of South America, trailing from mossy banks and ledges in humid shade.
plants.notes: String of Turtles has tiny, fleshy, circular leaves patterned like turtle shells on a fine trailing vine. It’s more prone to rot than most other peperomias, so the top layer of substrate should dry slightly between waterings — consistent dampness will kill it.

plants.toxicity: Contains calcium oxalate crystals; causes oral irritation and digestive upset if ingested. Toxic to cats and dogs.
plants.nativeContext: Native to the tropical rainforests of Southeast Asia from Thailand to Borneo, where juvenile plants shingle up tree trunks in the understorey.
plants.notes: Satin Pothos has matte, dark green heart-shaped leaves covered in reflective silver splotches. Juvenile growth stays compact and manageable in a terrarium. It needs good airflow though, so a fully sealed lid is a poor fit — a tall enclosure with vents at the top suits it well.

plants.toxicity: Berries are toxic to pets if ingested; repeated contact with fine foliage can cause skin irritation. Toxic to cats and dogs.
plants.nativeContext: Native to the coastal scrub and forest margins of southern Africa, scrambling through shrubs with hook-like branchlets.
plants.notes: Asparagus Fern is not a true fern but a lily-family member with feathery, horizontal cloud-like [[frond|fronds]] that create a convincing illusion of scale in large vessels. It grows vigorously and will outgrow most small or closed builds within a year or two.

plants.nativeContext: From rocky open hillsides and coastal scrubland in New Zealand and subtropical southern Africa — far more drought-tolerant than typical ferns.
plants.notes: Button Fern has leathery, round leaflets carried on arching dark-burgundy stems. Unlike most ferns, it tolerates lower humidity and brief dry periods without collapsing, which makes it one of the more reliable ferns for open terrariums and mixed setups where humidity is not consistent.

plants.nativeContext: From the humid, deeply shaded understorey of montane rainforests in southern Mexico and Guatemala.
plants.notes: Parlor Palm is typically sold as multi-stem seedlings that look exactly like a miniature palm grove. It grows very slowly, giving years of use in a tall, open container before it outgrows the space. It’s sensitive to fluoride in tap water, which will cause leaves to brown.

plants.nativeContext: A cosmopolitan tropical plant of humid forest floors and moist grasslands across Asia and West Africa, thriving where temperatures stay above 20 °C.
plants.notes: Little Tree Plant is a single-stemmed accent that looks exactly like a tiny palm tree, with leaflets that fold shut when touched or at dusk. It’s short-lived, typically declining after flowering, but it sets seed readily in a closed container and self-renews if conditions stay stable.

plants.nativeContext: A xeric epiphyte native to the seasonally dry forests and scrublands of Mexico, Guatemala, and Honduras, clinging to tree branches and rock faces with no soil contact.
plants.notes: Air Plant needs no substrate and can be wedged into cork bark or tucked into stone crevices without any potting at all. It should be misted 2–3 times a week and allowed to dry completely between mistings — standing water in the base of the rosette will rot the plant.

plants.nativeContext: Bred from Neoregelia species native to the Atlantic rainforest of Brazil; miniature cultivars developed specifically for use in enclosed vivariums.
plants.notes: Miniature Neoregelia can be mounted on driftwood, which lets the blushing, water-filled central cup be displayed prominently. The cup should be kept topped up with water and flushed and refilled weekly to prevent stagnation. It produces [[offset|offshoots]] freely after flowering, so the clump expands on its own.

plants.nativeContext: A neotropical liana from the humid rainforests of Central and South America, clinging and climbing tree trunks in deep understorey shade.
plants.notes: Marcgravia rectiflora is the most sought-after terrarium [[shingling|shingler]], pressing rounded juvenile leaves flat against cork, glass, or wood as it climbs toward the light. The shingling habit only develops when the stem is in direct contact with a vertical surface.

plants.nativeContext: A Southeast Asian epiphyte native to the Philippines and surrounding islands, clinging to tree branches in humid tropical forest canopies.
plants.notes: Million Hearts Plant is an epiphytic vine with tiny, stiff, heart-shaped leaves that are slightly succulent in texture. Does not like [[wet-feet|wet feet]], and can be mounted on branches or cork bark in a high-humidity build since the thickened leaves store enough moisture to tolerate brief gaps in watering.

plants.toxicity: Ficus sap (a milky latex) can irritate skin and the mouth; ingestion may cause mild digestive upset. Toxic to cats and dogs.
plants.nativeContext: A dwarf cultivar of a species native to East Asia, selected for smaller leaf scale and less aggressive growth than the standard creeping fig.
plants.notes: Panama Creeping Fig is a micro-leaved cultivar of Ficus pumila that forms a dense, delicate mat over backgrounds and substrate. It’s far less vigorous than the standard species, which makes it much safer for small builds with limited space.

plants.toxicity: An aroid containing insoluble calcium oxalate crystals; ingestion causes oral irritation, drooling, and vomiting. Toxic to cats and dogs.
plants.nativeContext: A rare aroid from the rainforests of Papua New Guinea and Queensland, Australia, where it presses its leaves flat against tree trunks in humid understorey.
plants.notes: Shingle Vine presses flat, alternating oval leaves tight against any textured vertical surface as it climbs toward the light. The [[shingling]] habit only develops when the stem is in contact with that surface — cork bark is the most reliable backing, and a plant left to trail loose won’t shingle at all.

plants.nativeContext: Native to the humid tropical forests of Southeast Asia, from Sri Lanka to Japan, growing on shaded forest floors and among mossy rocks.
plants.notes: Heart Fern is a dwarf fern with thick, glossy, heart-shaped [[frond|fronds]] that are quite unlike most ferns in texture and form. It’s highly sensitive to dry air — exposure to low humidity even briefly causes fronds to crisp at the edges and collapse. This means a sealed enclosure is non-negotiable.

plants.nativeContext: From the humid rainforests of Thailand and Southeast Asia, growing as an epiphyte on shaded tree trunks and mossy forest floors.
plants.notes: Blue Oil Fern develops a metallic blue-teal iridescence that appears under low light in high humidity — it’s one of the more dramatic color effects in terrarium keeping. The blue sheen fades with too much light or insufficient moisture, so dim conditions and consistent dampness are both required to maintain it.

plants.nativeContext: A cultivar of the Boston fern, originating from the humid tropical forests of the Americas; selected for heavily divided, feathered fronds.
plants.notes: Fluffy Ruffles Fern is a heavily feathered, dwarf cultivar of sword fern that grows in tight, upright rosettes. It stays compact for longer than most sword ferns, making it one of the more practical choices for humid builds where space is limited.

plants.nativeContext: A compact cultivar bred from a species native to the tropical Americas, selected for miniature size and chartreuse coloration.
plants.notes: Pixie Lime Peperomia is a dwarf peperomia with light chartreuse leaves that stays under 10 cm even at maturity. It handles the moisture levels of a closed terrarium better than most peperomias, without the rot risk that makes other species unreliable in sealed builds.

plants.nativeContext: A cultivar of a species native to the humid lowland forests of Ecuador and Peru, selected for striking silver-and-crimson variegation.
plants.notes: Piccolo Banda Peperomia has thick, slightly succulent, metallic silvery-green leaves with deep purple-crimson veining. Its succulent leaves rot quickly in soggy substrate, so the mix should be allowed to partially dry between waterings.

plants.nativeContext: A rare species from the humid rainforests of Borneo, growing in near-constant high humidity deep in forest understories.
plants.notes: Butterfly Begonia has narrow, translucent green leaves covered in vivid crimson spots. It needs the stable humidity of a sealed glass enclosure — open air will cause it to decline quickly, and the decline is difficult to reverse once it starts.

plants.nativeContext: A species from the humid tropical rainforests of Southeast Asia, naturally trailing over mossy tree trunks and forest floor debris.
plants.notes: Oak Leaf Miniature Begonia is a tiny trailing begonia with oak-shaped leaves that naturally climbs mossy wood and branches. It flowers readily with small pink blooms under high humidity. It does best when anchored against textured hardscape in a sealed build, where it can creep upward on its own.

plants.toxicity: An aroid containing insoluble calcium oxalate crystals. All parts are toxic if ingested, causing oral irritation, drooling, and vomiting. Toxic to cats and dogs.
plants.nativeContext: A compact 'Jewel Alocasia' from the deep shade of rainforest floors in Borneo, where it grows in consistently high humidity.
plants.notes: Black Velvet Alocasia has near-black, velvety leaves with stark white veins that show up dramatically even under low light. It grows more slowly than most aroids, which makes it manageable in medium-to-large closed builds. The substrate surface should approach dryness before watering — the [[corm]] sits at the soil line and rots in consistently soggy conditions.

plants.nativeContext: A cosmopolitan terrestrial moss found on bare soil, rock, and disturbed ground across tropical and subtropical regions worldwide.
plants.notes: Star Moss forms short, dense clumps that radiate outward in a star pattern when viewed from above. It works well for filling the gaps between rocks and hardscape where a taller groundcover would not fit.

plants.nativeContext: A native North American spikemoss found in moist meadows, marshes, and forest edges from Eastern Canada to the Gulf Coast.
plants.notes: Meadow Spikemoss is a low, flat-growing spikemoss that forms a lush, light-green turf layer over substrate. It needs constant dampness and shade to stay healthy, and works well as a ground-cover alternative to moss in bog and closed tropical builds where conditions are consistently wet.

plants.nativeContext: A semi-arid dwarf shrub native to the Eastern Cape of South Africa, where it forms dense thickets on rocky slopes and is browsed extensively by elephants.
plants.notes: Elephant Bush is a woody-stemmed succulent that works well as a structural mini-tree in desert and arid landscape builds. It responds well to [[pinching]] and shaping, tolerates significant neglect, and grows back quickly from hard cuts. It needs a fast-draining mineral mix and full sun — without both, it will [[etiolation|etiolate]] and lose its form.

plants.toxicity: Contains calcium oxalate crystals; causes mouth and throat irritation if ingested. Toxic to cats and dogs.
plants.nativeContext: From the humid tropical rainforests of Borneo (Kalimantan), Indonesia, growing on shaded forest floors in deep leaf litter.
plants.notes: Silver Dragon Alocasia has striking, textured silver-gray leaves with dark raised [[venation]] that gives the surface a sculptural quality. It needs high humidity and an extremely well-draining mix — the [[corm]] base is prone to rot if it sits in moisture for any length of time.

plants.toxicity: Contains calcium oxalate crystals; causes mouth and throat irritation if ingested. Toxic to cats and dogs.
plants.nativeContext: Alocasia × amazonica is a nursery hybrid of uncertain Southeast Asian parentage; 'Bambino' is a dwarf selection bred for compact indoor display.
plants.notes: Bambino Alocasia is a dwarf form with arrow-shaped, dark green leaves and bold silver-white veins. It stays manageable in a closed build but needs excellent drainage and airflow around the soil line to prevent crown rot, which is the most common way to lose this plant.

plants.toxicity: Contains calcium oxalate crystals; causes mouth and throat irritation if ingested. Toxic to cats and dogs.
plants.nativeContext: Native to the tropical forests of Thailand and peninsular Malaysia; the thick, heavily textured 'maharani' form was selected for its exceptional dragon-scale leaf surface.
plants.notes: Grey Dragon Alocasia has ultra-thick, rigid leaves with pronounced dragon-scale ridge texture and a metallic gray-green coloration that looks almost artificial. It’s more tolerant of lower humidity than most jewel alocasias, but it still demands excellent drainage — soggy substrate will rot the [[corm]] just as readily.

plants.nativeContext: Common and abundant in moist, shaded forest floors across eastern North America, carpeting soil, logs, and stumps.
plants.notes: Fern Moss spreads in flat, lacy sheets with distinctive branching that closely mimics tiny fern [[frond|fronds]]. It’s one of the most popular terrarium mosses for naturalistic forest builds. It knits quickly over substrate and hardscape and tolerates the high humidity of a sealed lid without any trouble.

plants.nativeContext: Native to moist streamsides and pond margins in Japan and temperate East Asia, Acorus gramineus grows as a semi-aquatic grass-like perennial in cool to subtropical climates. 'Chacosekisho' is a Japanese cultivar prized for its vivid gold-and-green striped foliage.
plants.notes: Chacosekisho Sweet Flag does best with roots consistently wet or partially submerged, which makes it well-suited to the base of a closed terrarium. It prefers cooler temperatures than most tropical species — prolonged heat above 25°C will cause it to decline. It’s slow-growing and stays compact.

plants.nativeContext: Native to the tropical understorey of Southeast Asia, Hemigraphis alternata grows as a low trailing herb on moist forest floors and shaded slopes. Its leaves display silvery-green metallic tops and rich deep-purple undersides.
plants.notes: Purple Waffle Plant thrives in the warm, humid confines of a closed terrarium, where its trailing habit adds ground-level color. The substrate should be kept evenly moist but not waterlogged. The purple coloration is most vivid under bright indirect light — if the light drops too low, the leaves revert to plain green.

plants.nativeContext: Native to slow-moving rivers, streams, and flooded margins in India, Hygrophila pinnatifida is an emergent aquatic plant with distinctively pinnate, fern-like leaves and reddish-brown undersides.
plants.notes: Hygrophila pinnatifida is excellent for terrarium builds where it can be planted in wet substrate for emersed growth. Emersed leaves are the largest and most ornate, but they need very high ambient humidity to develop fully — without it, the leaves stay small and undistinguished. Iron-rich substrate or supplemental iron dosing encourages the signature reddish coloration.

plants.nativeContext: Native to the tropical forests of Brazil, Ruellia makoyana grows in warm, humid understorey conditions. It produces velvety dark-green leaves marked with silver veining and bears bright pink tubular flowers.
plants.notes: Monkey Plant is best suited to large closed terrariums where its potential height has room to develop. Regular stem [[pinching]] keeps it bushy and compact rather than leggy. The velvety leaves will rot if misted directly, so the plant relies on ambient humidity rather than overhead watering.

plants.nativeContext: Native to the tropical forests of Myanmar, Strobilanthes dyerianus thrives in warm, humid environments with bright filtered light. Its iridescent silver-purple foliage is produced by microscopic light-diffracting structures on the leaf surface.
plants.notes: Persian Shield grows too large and vigorous for most enclosed terrariums and works better as an open container accent plant. It needs bright indirect light to maintain its iridescent purple coloration — in low light the leaves revert to dull green and most of the visual interest is lost. Good airflow around the broad leaves helps prevent the fungal issues they’re prone to in still, humid conditions.

plants.nativeContext: Native to rocky slopes and scrublands of South Africa and Zimbabwe, Ceropegia woodii grows as a semi-succulent trailing vine, storing water in its [[caudex]] and heart-shaped leaves.
plants.notes: String of Hearts needs deep watering followed by the substrate drying out completely before the next watering — overwatering is the main reason this plant fails. It trails naturally over the edge of an open bowl or shelf planter. The silver marbling on the heart-shaped leaves becomes more pronounced with brighter light.

plants.nativeContext: A selected form of Ceropegia woodii from southern Africa, the String of Spades produces darker foliage with more prominently spade-shaped leaves compared to the standard heart-leaf form.
plants.notes: String of Spades is a variant of Ceropegia woodii with a darker leaf color and more pronounced spade shape, and its care is identical to the standard species. The substrate should dry out completely between waterings, and bright light is needed to maintain the leaf color. It works well in open builds.

plants.nativeContext: Alocasia baginda is endemic to the limestone karst forests of Borneo, where it grows as an understorey plant in high humidity and filtered light. The 'Dragon Scale' cultivar is prized for its dark green leaves with dramatic silver-gray venation resembling reptilian scales.
plants.notes: Dragon Scale Alocasia needs consistently high humidity — if it drops below 70%, the leaf edges start to brown and crisp, and that damage is permanent. The mix should drain very well to prevent [[rhizome]] rot while still keeping moisture available to the roots. Juvenile plants are compact and suit larger terrariums reasonably well, but mature specimens will outgrow most enclosed builds.

plants.nativeContext: Native to tropical West and Central Africa, Anubias hastifolia inhabits shallow rivers, streams, and flooded forest margins. It produces large arrowhead-shaped leaves and grows emersed or submerged, anchoring its rhizome to rocks and submerged wood.
plants.notes: The [[rhizome]] of Hastifolia Anubias must never be buried. It should be attached to hardscape above the soil and left to send roots down on its own. It’s one of the larger Anubias species, which makes it best suited to spacious rather than small builds. Growth is extremely slow, but once established it’s very robust.

plants.nativeContext: A cultivar of Epipremnum aureum, which originates from tropical islands of French Polynesia and has naturalised widely across tropical Asia and the Pacific. 'Global Green' displays chartreuse and mid-green marbling throughout the leaf blade.
plants.notes: Global Green Pothos is one of the most forgiving terrarium vines, tolerating lower light and irregular watering better than most aroids. It can be trained up cork bark or moss poles, which encourages faster growth and larger leaves. In closed builds, regular trimming is important because it will crowd out smaller plants if left unchecked.

plants.nativeContext: A hybrid Philodendron of uncertain parentage, 'Burle Marx Fantasy' exhibits elongated, lance-shaped leaves with distinctive silver-blue blotching and a matte texture. As a hemiepiphyte, it produces aerial roots and climbs trees in its native tropical forest habitat.
plants.notes: Burle Marx Fantasy Philodendron produces larger leaves with more pronounced silver markings when given a moss pole or cork bark for its aerial roots to grip. High humidity is important, and cold draughts can cause significant damage, so it should be kept away from windows or vents.

plants.nativeContext: Found in montane and lowland tropical forests across Ecuador, Peru, Colombia, and Costa Rica, Philodendron verrucosum is a hemiepiphytic climber. Its deeply velvety dark green leaves are laced with golden-yellow veining.
plants.notes: Velvet Leaf Philodendron needs humidity above 70% — if it drops below that, leaf edges brown and new growth hardens before it fully opens. A chunky substrate or climbing support that lets the aerial roots grip something vertical mimics its natural epiphytic habit.

plants.nativeContext: A compact form of Philodendron verrucosum from the same tropical montane and lowland forests of Ecuador, Peru, and Colombia. Retains the characteristic velvety texture and golden venation of the species but remains significantly smaller.
plants.notes: Mini Velvet Leaf Philodendron has the same care requirements as the standard species — humidity above 70%, a chunky well-draining substrate, and a climbing surface are all essential. The leaf size typically stays under 10 cm, which makes this form manageable in medium-sized closed terrariums where the standard species would quickly become too large.

plants.nativeContext: Philodendron 'Wend-Imbe' is a climbing hybrid of Central and South American parentage, producing elongated lanceolate leaves with a semi-glossy finish. It grows as a hemiepiphyte in warm, humid tropical forest conditions.
plants.notes: Wend-Imbe Philodendron is a vigorous climber well-suited to large terrariums with vertical hardscape backing that lets the aerial roots attach properly -- and encourages faster upward growth. It’s notably more tolerant of lower humidity than velvet-leaf species, which makes it a more practical choice for builds that can’t maintain consistently high levels.

plants.nativeContext: Endemic to Papua New Guinea, Rhaphidophora cryptantha is a shingling aroid that adheres its flat leaves tightly against tree bark as it climbs, with each leaf overlapping the next like roof shingles.
plants.notes: Rhaphidophora cryptantha needs a vertical surface to climb — cork bark tiles or a flat hardscape panel are ideal. Without something to press against, the leaves remain juvenile and small indefinitely. Humidity above 70% encourages the adhesive roots that lock the leaves flat against the backing surface.

plants.nativeContext: A variegated cultivar of Syngonium podophyllum, which is native to tropical rainforests from Mexico through to Bolivia and Brazil. The species grows as a hemiepiphytic climbing vine in warm, humid understorey conditions.
plants.notes: Albo Variegated Arrowhead Plant has white variegated sectors that lack chlorophyll and are more sensitive to direct light than the green portions — direct sun will scorch them. Consistent moisture without giving it [[wet-feet|wet feet]] keeps the plant healthy. Reversion to all-green growth can occur at any time; removing all-green shoots promptly helps maintain the variegated pattern.

plants.nativeContext: Native to the tropical understorey forests of Costa Rica and Panama, Syngonium rayii is a compact, slow-growing species with deep velvety dark-green leaves bisected by a bold silver midrib.
plants.notes: Silver Goosefoot Plant is one of the most desirable compact Syngonium species for closed terrariums — the velvety texture and striking silver [[midrib]] read best at close range, which a glass-sided build provides naturally. It needs higher humidity than common Syngonium cultivars, and its slow growth keeps it manageable in medium-sized builds without constant trimming.

plants.nativeContext: Native to the tropical forest floors of Borneo and Sarawak, growing in deep shade under dense rainforest canopy with consistently high humidity.
plants.notes: Begonia baik forms a compact rosette of glossy leaves adorned with silver spots, which makes it a prized specimen for display terrariums. The substrate should be kept evenly moist, and water should be kept off the leaves to prevent rot. It prefers the still, humid air of a closed terrarium rather than any direct airflow.

plants.nativeContext: Endemic to the cloud forests of New Guinea at higher elevations, where cool temperatures, persistent mist, and extremely high humidity prevail year-round.
plants.notes: Begonia bipinnatifida has deeply pinnately lobed, fern-like leaves that are quite unlike any other begonia. It needs very high humidity above 80% and cooler temperatures, which makes a large, stable closed terrarium the only realistic option. It’s sensitive to stagnant conditions, so gentle airflow within the enclosure is important — a small fan or regular ventilation helps.

plants.nativeContext: Native to the humid tropical forests of Malaysia and surrounding Southeast Asian regions, growing as a forest-floor rhizomatous plant in filtered light.
plants.notes: Burkill’s Begonia Silver Form is a terrarium classic prized for its intense silvery-green leaf coloration. Its rhizomatous growth makes propagation from leaf or stem cuttings very straightforward. A closed environment is essential for maintaining the high humidity that keeps the leaf color vivid and the plant vigorous.

plants.nativeContext: Found in the tropical rainforests of Central Africa, specifically Cameroon and Gabon, growing on the forest floor in deep shade with consistent humidity.
plants.notes: Cleopatra Begonia has metallic silver-green leaves with an iridescent sheen that make it a standout African begonia for terrarium display. It should be grown in a well-sealed closed terrarium — fluctuating humidity causes leaf edge browning, and the damage accumulates over time.

plants.nativeContext: Native to the humid lowland and mid-elevation tropical forests of Costa Rica and Panama, growing in shaded spots with consistent moisture.
plants.notes: Pond Lily Begonia is one of the most forgiving terrarium begonias, tolerating a wide range of light and moisture conditions without complaint. The distinctive peltate leaves — where the [[petiole]] attaches at the centre of the leaf rather than the edge — are easy to identify. It propagates readily from leaf cuttings placed on moist substrate.

plants.nativeContext: Native to the cloud forests of Ecuador at higher elevations, where cool misty conditions and consistently high humidity define its natural habitat.
plants.notes: Dodson's Begonia is a rare cloud-forest species that demands cooler temperatures and very high humidity. Temperatures above 24°C will cause rapid decline that is very difficult to reverse. It's best suited to an expertly maintained, large closed terrarium with stable conditions — this is not a beginner plant, and the environmental requirements leave little margin for error.

plants.nativeContext: Found in the tropical forests of Central Africa, this shrubby cane-like begonia grows in humid understorey conditions with dappled light.
plants.notes: Elaeagnus-leaf Begonia has elongated silvery leaves with a distinctive texture that give it an elegant appearance among African begonias. Its upright, cane-like growth suits taller terrariums, and regular pruning keeps it compact and encourages the bushier side growth that makes the plant look its best.

plants.nativeContext: Native to the subtropical and montane forests of Colombia and Venezuela, growing along stream banks and in humid forest understorey at mid-elevations.
plants.notes: Begonia foliosa var. foliosa has tiny oval leaflets densely packed on arching stems, creating a feathery, fern-like appearance quite unlike most begonias. It tolerates slightly cooler conditions than tropical species, and it's well-suited to large, well-ventilated closed terrariums where the arching stems have room to cascade naturally.

plants.nativeContext: The species Begonia glabra is native to tropical regions of the Americas, climbing and trailing through humid forest understorey and along tree trunks.
plants.notes: Begonia glabra 'Bronze' is an excellent vivarium vine that scrambles up backgrounds and cork bark, producing small glossy leaves with an attractive bronze sheen. It grows quickly and vigorously, so trim it regularly to keep it from taking over a build.

plants.nativeContext: Endemic to the tropical forests of Cameroon in West Africa, growing as a rhizomatous ground-cover plant on the humid forest floor.
plants.notes: Begonia klemmei is a rare West African species with distinctively textured leaves that add visual interest to closed terrarium plantings. It grows slowly but rewards patience — maintain consistently high humidity and avoid wetting the leaves directly.

plants.nativeContext: The species Begonia longiciliata is native to the humid tropical forests of Southeast Asia, growing in shaded, moist conditions on the forest floor.
plants.notes: Begonia longiciliata 'Sizemoreae' is defined by the long hair-like cilia fringing its leaf margins, giving the leaves a fringed appearance unlike most begonias. A sealed closed terrarium is essential — the delicate leaf margins deteriorate quickly if humidity drops.

plants.nativeContext: Native to the tropical forests of Central Africa, particularly Cameroon, growing on the humid forest floor in deep shade.
plants.notes: Begonia loranthoides sp. rhopalocarpa is a rare Cameroonian species suited to experienced terrarium keepers. It needs stable high humidity in a well-sealed closed terrarium and a well-draining, peat-rich substrate — keeping moisture consistent while avoiding root rot is the central challenge with this one.

plants.nativeContext: Endemic to Luzon in the Philippines, growing in the tropical humid forests at low to mid elevations in shaded understorey conditions.
plants.notes: Begonia luzonensis is a Philippine species with medium-sized pointed leaves and a glossy texture. It performs reliably in closed terrariums with consistent moisture and filtered light once established.

plants.nativeContext: Named for Manaus in the heart of the Amazon basin, Brazil, where it grows in the warm, extremely humid conditions of the tropical rainforest understorey.
plants.notes: Begonia manaus is a vigorous cane-type begonia that thrives in warm, humid closed terrariums mimicking Amazon basin conditions. Its fast growth means regular pruning is needed in smaller builds; it's better suited to large paludariums or tall terrariums where it has room to run.

plants.nativeContext: The species Begonia maurandiae is native to the montane cloud forests of Central America, growing as a climbing plant in cool, misty, high-humidity conditions.
plants.notes: Begonia maurandiae 'Blue' is a climbing cultivar with an unusual blue-purple iridescent tint to its foliage and similarly tinted flowers. It requires cooler montane conditions and very high humidity, so it's best placed in a large, well-ventilated closed terrarium with consistently cool temperatures.

plants.nativeContext: Endemic to the Philippines, growing on the forest floor of tropical humid forests in shaded, consistently moist conditions.
plants.notes: Begonia polliloensis is a compact rhizomatous begonia from the Philippines, suited to smaller closed terrariums. It's rare in cultivation and slow to establish — keep humidity stable and don't let the substrate dry out at any point during establishment.

plants.nativeContext: Native to the tropical forests of West Africa, particularly Sierra Leone and Liberia, where it forms trailing mats on the humid forest floor.
plants.notes: Begonia prismatocarpa is one of the best begonias for closed terrariums: tiny leaves, a trailing habit, and cheerful yellow flowers that bloom reliably under terrarium conditions. It's easy and forgiving, making it a good choice for anyone new to begonias in closed builds.

plants.nativeContext: Native to the tropical forests of Mexico and Central America, growing as a rhizomatous ground-cover plant in humid, shaded understorey conditions.
plants.notes: Begonia pustulata has deeply bullate, puckered dark green leaves with contrasting silver markings — one of the most visually dramatic terrarium begonias available. It's manageable for the impact it delivers; propagate from [[rhizome]] cuttings and keep humidity consistently high.

plants.nativeContext: Native to the tropical rainforests of Malaysia and Borneo, growing as a large-leaved rhizomatous plant on the humid, shaded forest floor.
plants.notes: Begonia raja produces large, velvety, heavily textured leaves covered in fine hairs and adorned with silver spots, making it a stunning statement plant for spacious closed terrariums. It needs a large build to accommodate leaf size, humidity above 70%, and care to avoid wetting the hairy foliage.

plants.nativeContext: Native to the tropical forests of Brazil, growing as a rhizomatous ground-cover plant in the humid, shaded understorey where light levels vary.
plants.notes: Begonia soli-mutata has leaves that display dramatically different colors and patterns depending on light exposure, which makes it one of the more fascinating begonias to observe over time. It's easy to grow and propagate, and suits a range of closed terrarium setups.

plants.nativeContext: The species Begonia sutherlandii is native to subtropical regions of South Africa and East Africa, growing in rocky montane grasslands and forest margins at altitude.
plants.notes: Begonia sutherlandii 'Saunders Legacy' is a tuberous begonia that dies back in winter and requires a distinct cool, dry rest period — unlike most terrarium begonias. It is not suited to enclosed humid environments; grow it in an open terrarium or cool greenhouse where its cheerful orange flowers and compact trailing habit can be fully appreciated.

plants.nativeContext: Native to the cloud forests of Ecuador, growing in cool, misty, high-altitude conditions with consistently high humidity and filtered light.
plants.notes: Begonia thelmae is a small cloud-forest fibrous begonia that requires cooler temperatures and very high humidity to thrive. Avoid temperatures above 24°C and keep humidity above 80%; it's best suited to a large, stable closed terrarium with a consistently cool environment.

plants.nativeContext: Named for Turrialba in Costa Rica, this begonia is native to the cloud forest understorey at mid-to-high elevations, where cool temperatures and persistent high humidity prevail.
plants.notes: Begonia turrialbae is a compact cloud-forest species suited to cool, humid closed terrariums. Keep temperatures in the 15–24°C range and humidity above 80% — this species is sensitive to fluctuations in both, and stress shows quickly.

plants.nativeContext: Native to the tropical forests of Central and South America, growing in humid understorey conditions across a range of elevations and forest types.
plants.notes: Begonia variabilis shows notable variation in leaf form between populations and under different growing conditions, which is part of what makes it interesting to collect. It's a reliable performer in closed terrariums, adapts to a range of light levels, and rewards consistent moisture and high humidity with steady growth.

plants.nativeContext: Terrestrial bromeliad native to Brazil, growing on the forest floor in dappled light.
plants.notes: Cryptanthus 'Absolute Zero' is a compact earth star with silvery-white striped leaves that holds minimal water in its central cup compared to tank bromeliads. It needs airflow — avoid sealed enclosures — and [[offset|offsets]] freely once established.

plants.nativeContext: Hybrid Neoregelia bred from Blushing Tiger and Puntatissima parent types, both native to Brazilian Atlantic Forest.
plants.notes: Neoregelia 'Blushing Tiger x Puntatissima' is a compact miniature hybrid with tiger-striped banding and a blushing pink center cup. Brighter light intensifies the coloration; keep the central tank filled with water and ensure good air circulation.

plants.nativeContext: Miniature Neoregelia hybrid, parentage tracing to Brazilian Atlantic Forest species.
plants.notes: Neoregelia 'Cotton Candy' has a soft pink suffusion across the foliage that intensifies under bright indirect light. It's a small compact rosette suited to open terrariums and tabletop displays; keep the central cup filled with rainwater or distilled water.

plants.nativeContext: Compact Neoregelia hybrid derived from Brazilian Atlantic Forest bromeliad species.
plants.notes: Neoregelia 'Easter Egg' has a mottled multi-tonal coloring across a small flat rosette that resembles decorated eggs. Keep the central cup filled with water and change it regularly to prevent stagnation.

plants.nativeContext: Popular compact Neoregelia cultivar, parentage from Brazilian Atlantic Forest species.
plants.notes: Neoregelia 'Fireball' is one of the most widely grown miniature Neoregelia cultivars. It turns brilliant crimson-red under high bright-indirect or some direct light, and stays green in lower light. It [[offset|offsets]] readily and forms clumping colonies over time.

plants.nativeContext: Hybrid between the miniature Lilyput and Pheasant Neoregelia types, parentage from Brazilian Atlantic Forest.
plants.notes: Neoregelia 'Lilyput x Pheasant' is a tiny rosette with spotted markings inherited from the Pheasant parent — one of the more diminutive Neoregelia hybrids. It's well-suited to small open terrarium arrangements; keep the central cup filled with clean water.

plants.nativeContext: Miniature Neoregelia hybrid with Brazilian Atlantic Forest bromeliad parentage.
plants.notes: Neoregelia 'Mini Skirt' has a broad, flat, spreading rosette with a wide low profile. Good air circulation is essential, so avoid enclosed terrariums; fill the central cup with rainwater or distilled water.

plants.nativeContext: Compact Neoregelia hybrid, parentage from Brazilian Atlantic Forest bromeliad species.
plants.notes: Neoregelia 'Mojo' has vibrant coloring that intensifies with brighter indirect light. Its compact growth habit suits open terrarium displays; keep the central tank filled and flush the cup periodically to prevent bacterial buildup.

plants.nativeContext: Specialist hybrid created by bromeliad hybridizer Dennis Skotak, parentage from Brazilian Atlantic Forest Neoregelia.
plants.notes: Neoregelia 'Skotak's Matilde' is a miniature collector cultivar with heavily patterned foliage, prized by specialist bromeliad collectors for its intricate markings and compact stature. It requires good airflow and is not suited to closed environments.

plants.nativeContext: Specialist hybrid by bromeliad hybridizer Dennis Skotak, parentage from Brazilian Atlantic Forest Neoregelia.
plants.notes: Neoregelia 'Skotak Mirage' is a miniature Skotak hybrid with patterned foliage displaying shifting tonal qualities, rarely seen outside specialist bromeliad circles. Airflow is essential — this is not a plant for closed terrariums.

plants.nativeContext: Unnamed hybrid by bromeliad specialist Dennis Skotak, parentage from Brazilian Atlantic Forest Neoregelia species.
plants.notes: Neoregelia 'Skotak's Unregistered' is an unnamed, unregistered Skotak hybrid circulating among specialist collectors, with the miniature stature and distinctive patterning typical of the Skotak line. Label it as 'Skotak's Unregistered' to distinguish it from named siblings; airflow is required.

plants.nativeContext: Compact Neoregelia hybrid with Brazilian Atlantic Forest bromeliad parentage.
plants.notes: Neoregelia 'Wild Tiger Goode' has distinctive tiger-striped transverse banding across the leaf blades. The bold patterning becomes more pronounced with adequate bright indirect light; keep the central cup filled with clean water.

plants.nativeContext: Tropical rainforest floor of Peru and Ecuador, growing in deep shade with consistently high humidity.
plants.notes: Geogenanthus poeppigii, the Seersucker Plant, has distinctive quilted, seersucker-textured dark green leaves with silver banding. It demands very high humidity and will desiccate quickly in open environments — sealed or near-sealed bioactive terrariums are ideal. It's sensitive to cold drafts and dry air.

plants.nativeContext: Widespread across tropical Central and South America in shallow water, marsh edges, and wet meadows. Can grow emersed or submerged.
plants.notes: Eleocharis radicans is a fine grass-like sedge that creates a dense carpet effect when grown emersed in a paludarium or high-humidity closed terrarium with a wet substrate. It spreads by [[stolon|stolons]] to form a mat; the roots must remain wet at all times and the substrate should never dry out.

plants.nativeContext: Northeast Queensland, Australia and Papua New Guinea; grows in humid gullies and stream margins in subtropical conditions.
plants.notes: Drosera adelae, the Lance-Leaf Sundew, has narrow strap-like leaves densely covered in glistening red tentacles and is one of the easier tropical sundews to keep. Use only pure water — rainwater, RO, or distilled — because minerals from tap water will kill it; the tray method works well. Never fertilize the substrate.

plants.nativeContext: Extremely widespread — Australia, New Zealand, Japan, Southeast Asia; grows in bogs, seepages, and wet acidic soils from sea level to montane zones.
plants.notes: Drosera spatulata, the Spoon-Leaf Sundew, is one of the most adaptable and beginner-friendly sundews — a compact flat rosette of spoon-shaped leaves covered in sticky red glands. It self-seeds readily in suitable conditions, tolerates a wide temperature range, and needs only pure water in the tray.

plants.nativeContext: Cloud forests and montane wetlands of the Andes — Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Bolivia — at elevations of 2000-4000m.
plants.notes: Equisetum bogotense, the Andean Horsetail, is a delicate fine-stemmed species quite unlike the robust common Equisetum arvense. It requires cool intermediate temperatures and will suffer above 22°C, so it's suited only to closed cool terrariums with extremely high humidity. Once established it can spread vigorously via [[rhizome|rhizomes]].

plants.nativeContext: Tropical forests of Thailand and Malaysia; epiphytic trailing habit on mossy branches and rocky outcrops.
plants.notes: Aeschynanthus longicaulis 'Black Pagoda' has waxy dark green leaves with distinctive marbled lighter undersides and produces orange-yellow tubular flowers. Trailing stems can reach 60 cm; allow the soil to approach dryness between waterings to avoid root rot, and provide bright indirect light to encourage flowering.

plants.nativeContext: Tropical forests of Mexico and Central America; stoloniferous creeping herb on forest floors and rocky banks.
plants.notes: Alsobia dianthiflora, the Lace Flower Vine, produces distinctive white tubular flowers with heavily fringed petal margins. It spreads readily by [[stolon|stolons]] and makes an excellent terrarium ground cover; avoid waterlogging and ensure good drainage. It's sensitive to cold drafts.

plants.nativeContext: Epiphytic in rainforests of Costa Rica, Panama, and northern South America; often grows in association with ant nests.
plants.notes: Codonanthe carnosa has small, thick, waxy leaves with a somewhat succulent appearance and produces white flowers with a red-streaked throat. Its semi-succulent leaves let it tolerate slightly drier conditions than most gesneriads, but good drainage is still essential — never let it sit in water.

plants.nativeContext: Epiphytic rainforest habitats of Central and South America; typically found on tree branches and in humid forest understory.
plants.notes: Codonanthe digna has smaller leaves than C. carnosa and a more compact trailing growth habit, with white flowers marked in yellow and red. It grows more slowly and is slightly more demanding than its relative; keep it moderately moist with excellent drainage.

plants.nativeContext: Limestone karst outcrops and cave margins in northern Vietnam; adapted to bright but diffuse light conditions on rocky substrates.
plants.notes: Deinostigma tamiana, formerly Chirita tamiana, forms a compact rosette with soft textured leaves and produces dainty white flowers with purple striping in the throat. It's ideal for nano terrariums, prefers lean, well-draining substrate, and the leaves should be kept dry — don't mist them directly.

plants.nativeContext: Cultivar derived from Central and South American Episcia species; parent species grow on tropical forest floors and as epiphytes.
plants.notes: Episcia 'Chocolate Velvet' has velvety chocolate-brown leaves with contrasting silvery markings and produces orange-red tubular flowers. It spreads by [[stolon|stolons]] to form a ground-covering mat, but is extremely sensitive to cold air and low humidity — a single cold draft can cause leaf damage. Never mist the leaves directly.

plants.nativeContext: Cultivar derived from Central and South American Episcia species; parent species inhabit humid tropical forest understory.
plants.notes: Episcia 'Lilacina' has silver-green patterned foliage and produces lilac to lavender flowers, unusual in the Episcia genus. It spreads by [[stolon|stolons]] and requires consistently high humidity and warmth; any temperature drop or dry air will cause rapid deterioration.

plants.nativeContext: Cultivar derived from Central and South American Episcia species; parent species grow in humid tropical forest understory.
plants.notes: Episcia 'Silver Dust' has heavily silver-dusted foliage that gives it a frosted appearance, with contrasting bright red tubular flowers. Like all Episcias, it requires warm temperatures and very high humidity — cold and dry conditions are fatal, not just stressful.

plants.nativeContext: Humid tropical forests of South America, particularly Peru and Colombia; grows as a terrestrial herb in deep forest shade.
plants.notes: Nautilocalyx sp. is an erect-stemmed gesneriad with highly textured, bullate (puckered) leaves ranging from dark green to bronze or purple-tinted. It's a striking foliage plant even without flowers, thrives in low light, and needs consistently high humidity. Keep the substrate consistently moist but not waterlogged.

plants.nativeContext: Epiphytic and terrestrial rainforest habitats of Suriname and northern South America; humid tropical lowland forest.
plants.notes: Paradrymonia campostyla has trailing to pendant stems bearing large, attractively textured leaves — an uncommon gesneriad in cultivation with genuinely striking foliage. It requires consistently warm temperatures and high humidity, and a chunky, well-draining but moisture-retentive substrate works best for mimicking epiphytic conditions.

plants.nativeContext: Hybrid cultivar; parent species are native to Brazil and tropical South America, typically growing on rocky outcrops and in forest margins.
plants.notes: Sinningia 'Freckles' is a tiny tuberous gesneriad that forms a compact mound of small velvety leaves topped with spotted purple flowers. It goes dormant in winter — reduce watering significantly and keep the [[tuber]] dry until new growth emerges in spring. It's excellent for nano terrariums.

plants.nativeContext: Hybrid cultivar; parent species native to Brazil and tropical South America, growing on rocky outcrops and in shaded forest margins.
plants.notes: Sinningia 'Prudence Risley' is a classic miniature Sinningia for terrariums, producing violet-purple tubular flowers prolifically above compact foliage. It grows from a small [[tuber]] and may go dormant in low-light winter conditions — resume regular watering once new growth appears. A well-draining substrate is critical to prevent tuber rot.

plants.nativeContext: Widespread across tropical and subtropical Asia, Pacific islands, and Africa; grows on moist rock faces, tree bark, and soil in deeply shaded humid forests.
plants.notes: Crepidomanes minutum, the Minute Filmy Fern, has [[frond|fronds]] just a single cell layer thick and completely transparent. It will desiccate and die within hours if humidity drops below saturation — sealed or near-sealed terrariums with stable near-100% humidity are the only suitable environment. It can be attached to live moss or moist rock rather than planted in substrate, and grows extremely slowly.

plants.nativeContext: Wetlands, riverbanks, and shallow streams across peninsular India; grows both submerged and emergent.
plants.notes: Pogostemon deccanensis has fine, feathery whorled leaves that create a delicate texture and is widely used as an aquarium stem plant; it adapts well to emersed growth in paludariums and wet terrariums. It grows fast and will need regular trimming; plant it in a nutrient-rich, waterlogged substrate for best results.

plants.nativeContext: Tropical and montane forests of Asia and the Neotropics; typically epiphytic on mossy tree branches or terrestrial on humid forest floors.
plants.notes: Huperzia sp. is an ancient vascular plant — a lycophyte, not a true fern or moss — with pendant or upright tasseled stems densely clothed in small scale-like leaves. It's slow growing and sensitive to root disturbance; mounting on cork bark or tree fern fiber is better than planting in substrate. It needs consistently high humidity and good air circulation, and soggy substrate will kill it.

plants.nativeContext: An informally named Marcgravia morphotype from tropical South or Central America, circulated in the vivarium hobby for its warm bronze-tinted juvenile shingling leaves. Marcgravia species grow as hemi-epiphytic vines in humid tropical rainforest, pressing juvenile leaves flat against tree bark.
plants.notes: Marcgravia sp. 'Bronze' can be mounted on cork bark or a coir panel within a high-humidity vivarium, where its juvenile leaves will [[shingling|shingle]] flat to the surface as it climbs. Mount it vertically only — sitting in wet soil will cause decline. Even brief desiccation causes leaf drop, so stable humidity and temperature are non-negotiable.

plants.nativeContext: An informally named Marcgravia morphotype traded in the vivarium hobby, distinguished by dark brown to near-black juvenile foliage. As with all Marcgravia, it is a hemi-epiphytic rainforest vine from tropical America.
plants.notes: Marcgravia sp. 'Dark Brown' requires the same conditions as other hobby Marcgravia forms: a high-humidity vivarium, a vertical cork or coir mounting surface, no dry spells. Its deep coloration makes it a striking background species, but plan for the long haul — meaningful coverage takes months to years.

plants.nativeContext: A Marcgravia morphotype or undescribed species collected from Ecuador's tropical lowland or foothill rainforest, circulated in the specialist vivarium hobby. Juvenile leaves are typically small, round, and press flat to bark in classic shingling fashion.
plants.notes: Marcgravia sp. 'Ecuador' needs a vertical cork bark mounting surface, ultra-high humidity, and stable warmth. Progress is slow by any measure — [[shingling]] marcgravias advance only a few centimeters per month under good conditions. Don't let humidity drop below 80% or the roots dry out.

plants.nativeContext: A Marcgravia form originating from the El Coca region of the Ecuadorian Amazon — a zone of exceptional biodiversity in the western Amazon basin. Collected and traded as a shingling background vine for vivaria; exact species status is unresolved.
plants.notes: Marcgravia sp. 'El Coca' follows the same basic requirements as all hobby Marcgravia: a high-humidity enclosed vivarium, a vertical mounting surface, warmth, and patience. The El Coca region spans lowland through foothill elevations, so this form may tolerate a slight temperature spread, but consistent warmth produces the best growth.

plants.nativeContext: A Marcgravia form collected from the Peruvian Amazon or Andean foothills, traded in the vivarium hobby as a shingling background vine. Peru has exceptional Marcgravia diversity across its lowland and cloud-forest zones.
plants.notes: Marcgravia sp. 'Peru' can be mounted vertically in a closed high-humidity vivarium and is well-suited to the back wall of a large paludarium or rainforest terrarium. Like all [[shingling]] vines, it needs enough vertical run — a cramped enclosure will stall growth quickly.

plants.nativeContext: A Caribbean Marcgravia form from Puerto Rico's montane rainforest, where several Marcgravia species grow as epiphytic vines in the island's humid interior forests. The Caribbean forms are distinct from South American material in subtle leaf and stem characters.
plants.notes: Marcgravia sp. 'Puerto Rico' grows well in closed vivaria; being from a Caribbean island, it may tolerate slightly lower minimum humidity than deep Amazon forms, but still needs well above 80%. It can serve as a shingling back-wall vine in a tropical paludarium build.

plants.nativeContext: A Marcgravia form originating from St Lucia in the Lesser Antilles, where it grows epiphytically in the island's rain-forested peaks. Island endemic forms can differ meaningfully from mainland species in leaf shape and stem coloring.
plants.notes: Marcgravia sp. 'St Lucia Island' follows the standard closed-vivarium [[shingling]] care protocol. Because St Lucia's climate is consistently warm and humid year-round, this form may be especially intolerant of temperature fluctuations — keep conditions stable above 20°C.

plants.nativeContext: A described Marcgravia species from the Caribbean and northern South America region. Like other members of the genus it climbs tree trunks in humid rainforest, producing clasping juvenile leaves and pendant adult inflorescences with cup-like nectaries that attract bats for pollination.
plants.notes: Marcgravia sintinesii requires the same high-humidity, high-warmth closed conditions as all Marcgravia. It's one of the more structurally variable species as it transitions from its [[shingling]] juvenile to free-hanging adult phase — a mature specimen in a large vivarium is a showpiece, but allow adequate vertical height for that to happen.

plants.nativeContext: A Marcgravia form from the lowland tropical rainforest of Suriname in the Guiana Shield region of northeastern South America — a hotspot of endemism. Suriname's Marcgravia flora is not fully described, so this hobbyist name likely covers an undescribed taxon.
plants.notes: Marcgravia sp. 'Suriname' comes from Suriname's hot, humid lowlands and prefers slightly warmer minimums than Andean or Caribbean forms. Keep the vivarium consistently above 22°C and humidity near saturation; mount it vertically on cork.

plants.nativeContext: Marcgravia umbellata is a widespread Marcgravia species from the Caribbean, Central, and South America. The 'Red' form is selected for its vivid reddish flush on new growth and stems, making it more ornamental than the typical green form.
plants.notes: Marcgravia umbellata 'Red' is one of the more adaptable species in the genus and the most widely cultivated. The red coloration is most intense under brighter light and with good nutrient availability. Standard high-humidity closed vivarium conditions apply, and it grows larger than most other hobby Marcgravia when given space.
