plants.nativeContext: Calvoa sessiliflora is a small herbaceous member of the Melastomataceae native to the humid understorey of tropical West and Central African rainforests. It grows as a low-light specialist on permanently moist forest floors, producing attractively veined foliage.
plants.notes: Calvoa sessiliflora is a challenging West African terrarium plant suited only to specialist tropical enclosures. Consistently high humidity and warmth are required at all times — any dryness or cold is fatal. Keep it in low to medium light on moist, well-aerated substrate; it's rarely available in cultivation, so source it from specialist dealers.

plants.nativeContext: Monolena primulaeflora is a compact, rosette-forming member of the Melastomataceae from the tropical rainforests of South America, particularly Ecuador and Peru. The 'Red' form is selected for vivid red-purple undersides and suffused upper leaf coloring, making it highly ornamental.
plants.notes: Monolena primulaeflora 'Red' is a stunning but demanding plant that requires consistently saturated humidity and warmth. Plant it in a shallow-rooted mix of fine peat and sphagnum and never let it dry out. The red coloration intensifies in slightly brighter light, but direct exposure will cause burning — a mature, stable closed terrarium is the only reliable environment.

plants.nativeContext: An ornamental Sonerila form from Southeast Asia, likely from montane rainforest in Thailand, Vietnam, or Myanmar. Sonerila species grow as low-light herb-layer plants in cool, humid forest understorey. The 'Neon Lights' name refers to iridescent blue-green sheen on the upper leaf surface.
plants.notes: Sonerila sp. 'Neon Lights' produces an iridescent blue sheen via specialized cell structures that reflect blue wavelengths, most vivid under moderate indirect light at high humidity. Avoid misting the foliage directly — water marks are highly visible on these leaves. Keep the substrate consistently moist, and note that this species does best in cool to intermediate closed terrariums.

plants.nativeContext: Sonerila cantonensis is native to southern China and Vietnam, growing in shaded, moist limestone and clay soils in forested hills. The Vietnam form is noted in the hobby for particularly vivid metallic leaf patterning.
plants.notes: Sonerila cantonensis from Vietnam prefers slightly cooler temperatures than typical tropical species — intermediate conditions of 16–25°C suit it well. Keep humidity high and the substrate consistently moist; its small size makes it an excellent candidate for a cool, closed mossy terrarium or a nano-scale build.

plants.nativeContext: Ficus thunbergii is a compact, small-leaved fig native to East Asia, used in traditional Japanese bonsai practice. It grows naturally on rocky hillsides and forest margins in Japan, Korea, and China, producing small rounded to oval leaves and a distinctive aerial root system.
plants.notes: Ficus thunbergii is an excellent choice for a naturalistic bonsai terrarium or a large paludarium where its aerial roots can be displayed. It tolerates a range of light and temperature conditions but is less forgiving of saturated soil than many tropical figs, so keep the substrate moist but not waterlogged. A good candidate for cooler builds.

plants.nativeContext: Ficus vaccinioides is a miniature creeping fig endemic to Taiwan and parts of southern China, where it grows on moist rock faces and mossy tree bases in cool montane forests. Its leaves are tiny (2–5 mm) and round, making it one of the smallest-leaved figs in cultivation.
plants.notes: Ficus vaccinioides is highly sought after for nano terrariums because of its extraordinarily small leaves. It grows as a tight creeping mat, ideal for covering hardscape or filling the ground layer, and it's capable of growing on bark or rock as an epiphyte. It prefers slightly cooler temperatures than tropical figs; keep it moist and humid, as the tiny leaves desiccate rapidly.

plants.nativeContext: Ficus villosa is a climbing fig from Southeast Asia — ranging through Thailand, Malaysia, Borneo, and the Philippines — where it climbs tree trunks and rocky outcrops in humid tropical and montane forests. Its leaves are notably hairy (villous) giving a distinctive velvety texture.
plants.notes: Ficus villosa is distinguished by its hairy leaves, which press flat to bark in the juvenile [[shingling]] phase and work well covering the background of a vivarium. High humidity helps keep the leaf hairs clean and healthy. It's more tolerant of lower humidity than Marcgravia but still does best in a closed environment.

plants.nativeContext: Ficus punctata is a shingling/climbing fig from Southeast Asia (Malaysia, Indonesia, Philippines) that grows epiphytically on tree trunks in humid tropical rainforest. Its juvenile leaves press flat to bark and are punctuated with small dot-like structures, giving it its name.
plants.notes: Ficus punctata is a fast-[[shingling]] background vine once established, capable of creating a dense covering on cork bark. It needs a vertical surface, high humidity, and bright indirect light for best growth. Easier than Marcgravia, it produces waxy, pale green juvenile leaves with interesting textural detail.

plants.nativeContext: Ficus pumila 'Snowflake' is a variegated cultivar of the creeping fig, native to East Asia. The species climbs walls and tree trunks by means of adhesive root pads. The 'Snowflake' cultivar features irregular cream-white splashes and margins on its small rounded leaves.
plants.notes: Ficus pumila 'Snowflake' is one of the most manageable shingling figs for terrariums, more tolerant of temperature variation and lower humidity than tropical species. It grows vigorously once established, so trim it regularly. The variegated form grows slightly slower than the green type, and both can revert to plain green under low light — medium to bright indirect light preserves the variegation.

plants.nativeContext: Micranthemum tweedei, sold under the trade name 'Monte Carlo', is native to Argentina and Uruguay where it grows as a semi-aquatic carpet plant along stream margins and seasonally inundated areas. It is popular in aquascaping and emersed terrarium work.
plants.notes: Micranthemum tweedei, commonly known as Monte Carlo, forms a dense, low bright-green carpet when grown emersed in high-humidity terrariums. It needs a nutrient-rich fine substrate kept permanently saturated, making it excellent for the wet zone of a paludarium or the floor of a high-humidity closed build. Under bright light it stays compact; under low light, stems [[etiolation|etiolate]] and the carpet opens up.

plants.nativeContext: Anoectochilus albolineatus is a jewel orchid native to the humid understorey of tropical Southeast Asian forests, from peninsular Malaysia through to Borneo and Sumatra. It grows on damp, leaf-littered forest floors in deep shade, producing velvety dark leaves with striking white or silver veining.
plants.notes: Anoectochilus albolineatus is a jewel orchid grown primarily for its foliage rather than its flowers. Plant it in a shallow layer of fine-grade sphagnum or sphagnum-peat mix and maintain high, stable humidity without wetting the leaves directly. Rotting stems are the main failure mode — good air circulation is essential to prevent stagnant conditions despite the humidity, and the plant is very rewarding once it stabilizes.

plants.nativeContext: An Anoectochilus hybrid or cultivar circulated in the jewel orchid hobby as 'Charlotte's Web', noted for an intricate, lace-like pattern of silver veins on velvety dark foliage — reminiscent of spider silk. Anoectochilus naturally inhabit shaded, humid tropical forest floors across Southeast Asia.
plants.notes: Anoectochilus sp. 'Charlotte's Web' follows the same care as other jewel orchids in the genus: low light, fine sphagnum substrate, consistent moisture, high humidity, and no direct wetting of the leaves. The delicate vein pattern makes it a prized collector's piece; avoid disturbing established plants unnecessarily, as the roots are fragile.

plants.nativeContext: Anoectochilus formosanus is the Taiwanese jewel orchid, endemic to Taiwan's humid mountain forests at elevations of 500–2000 m. It has a long history in Chinese traditional medicine and is one of the more cold-tolerant Anoectochilus species.
plants.notes: Anoectochilus formosanus, the Formosan Jewel Orchid, is one of the easier species in the genus to maintain, tolerating intermediate temperatures and briefly down to 15°C. Keep it in sphagnum with stable moisture and indirect light. It's widely available and a good introduction to jewel orchid cultivation.

plants.nativeContext: Masdevallia auropurpurea is a cool-growing epiphytic orchid from the cloud forests of Colombia and Ecuador, typically found at elevations of 1800–2800 m. It produces dramatic flowers with gold-yellow inner coloration and purple-flushed sepaline tails.
plants.notes: Masdevallia auropurpurea is a cool-growing orchid that critically requires cool temperatures — sustained warmth above 22°C will kill it. A closed terrarium will overheat unless actively chilled, so it's best grown in a cool orchid cabinet or a cool-room terrarium with excellent ventilation. Plant it in fine bark-sphagnum mix and never let the roots dry completely; use rainwater or RO water, as water quality matters for this genus.

plants.nativeContext: Masdevallia floribunda is a compact, free-flowering cool-to-intermediate growing orchid from the cloud forests of Mexico, Guatemala, and Honduras at 1200–2500 m elevation. As the name suggests, it produces an abundance of small, delicate flowers throughout the year.
plants.notes: Masdevallia floribunda is one of the more intermediate-tolerant Masdevallia species and slightly more adaptable than most. It needs good air movement, consistently moist fine bark or sphagnum, and cool to intermediate temperatures. A cool, well-ventilated open terrarium or an orchid case suits it well; it will not tolerate stagnant warm air.

plants.nativeContext: Masdevallia nidifica is a cool-growing miniature orchid from the high cloud forests of Costa Rica, Panama, Colombia, and Ecuador at 1600–2800 m. The name 'nidifica' refers to the nest-like cluster of leaves. It produces small but charming pale flowers with elongated tails.
plants.notes: Masdevallia nidifica is a true cool-grower that suffers in any warmth above 22°C. It requires near-constant high humidity with outstanding air movement — the cloud forest conditions it comes from are literally foggy. It can be mounted on cork bark or grown in a well-vented cool case; its miniature size makes it ideal where space is limited but temperatures permit.

plants.nativeContext: Pleurothallis allenii is a miniature cool-to-intermediate epiphytic orchid from the cloud forests of Panama and Costa Rica. It grows in very high humidity on moss-covered branches in the mountain forest understorey.
plants.notes: Pleurothallis allenii is a miniature orchid well-suited to a cool, high-humidity terrarium with good airflow. It can be mounted on cork or grown in fine sphagnum, and it produces successive tiny flowers directly from the leaf. Keep the roots moist but never waterlogged; good air circulation prevents rot.

plants.nativeContext: Pleurothallis costaricensis is native to the montane cloud forests of Costa Rica where it grows as a miniature epiphyte in perpetually humid conditions. It produces a succession of tiny intricate flowers throughout the year directly from its flat, round leaves.
plants.notes: Pleurothallis costaricensis is a diminutive orchid suited to nano terrariums or small vivaria with intermediate to cool temperatures. Cork bark mounting allows the roots to breathe. Its flowers are tiny but appear consistently from the leaf [[axil]] and reward a closer look with a loupe. Never let the mount dry completely — it's sensitive to desiccation.

plants.nativeContext: Pleurothallis grobyi is one of the most widespread and vigorous miniature orchids, ranging from Mexico and the Caribbean through Central America to Bolivia and Brazil. It adapts well to intermediate conditions, making it one of the easier Pleurothallis species for beginners.
plants.notes: Pleurothallis grobyi is one of the most beginner-friendly miniature orchids, tolerating a wider temperature range than most Pleurothallis and growing vigorously once settled. It produces bright yellow-orange tiny flowers in clusters throughout the year, making it an excellent starter species for a high-humidity terrarium.

plants.nativeContext: Pleurothallis rubella is a miniature epiphytic orchid from the cloud forests of Central and South America. It grows in perpetually moist, shaded conditions and produces small reddish-pink flowers that appear from the leaf surface throughout the year.
plants.notes: Pleurothallis rubella is a small terrarium orchid suited to cool to intermediate conditions, with a reddish tint in the leaves and flowers that gives it distinctive appeal in a planted vivarium. Keep the roots moist on a cork or bark mount and ensure good airflow to prevent basal rot. Avoid temperature extremes.

plants.nativeContext: Restrepia lansburgii is a cool-to-intermediate growing epiphytic orchid from the Andes cloud forests of Colombia and Venezuela, found at 1500–2800 m elevation. Restrepia species produce a succession of colorful, intricate flowers from the leaf base throughout the year.
plants.notes: Restrepia lansburgii is an excellent candidate for a well-ventilated cool orchid terrarium — it stays compact and flowers continuously when conditions are right. Provide intermediate to cool temperatures (12–22°C), good airflow, and consistent moisture without waterlogging. It will not tolerate the heat of a closed tropics-style vivarium.

plants.nativeContext: Restrepia muscifera is a cool-growing Andean cloud forest orchid from Colombia, Venezuela, and Ecuador at 1500–2700 m. The name 'muscifera' (fly-bearing) refers to the fly-mimicking flower structure it uses to attract insect pollinators.
plants.notes: Restrepia muscifera follows the same care as other cool Restrepia: intermediate to cool temperatures, high humidity, and excellent air circulation. Its intricately patterned flowers mimic flies, which is genuinely fascinating, and it produces flowers at multiple times of year from leaf [[node|nodes]]. An ideal cool-case orchid for a specimen vivarium.

plants.nativeContext: Scaphosepalum dodsoni is a miniature cool-growing orchid from the cloud forests of Ecuador and Colombia at 1800–2500 m elevation. Scaphosepalum species are known for their unusual asymmetric flowers and their habit of producing blooms in sequence from a single long-lived inflorescence.
plants.notes: Scaphosepalum dodsoni requires cool cloud-forest conditions and should not exceed 22°C. Its single flower spike blooms repeatedly over months, opening one flower at a time — a fascinating trait to observe. It can be grown mounted or in small pots with excellent drainage and very high humidity with airflow. This is not a plant for tropical vivaria; it needs a cool case.

plants.nativeContext: Stelis argentata is a miniature epiphytic orchid from the montane cloud forests of Central and South America, found from Mexico to Bolivia at 1000–2800 m. It produces tiny, intricate flowers arranged in dense rows along slender spikes, often with a silvery sheen.
plants.notes: Stelis argentata is among the most compact and container-friendly of the miniature orchids. It can be mounted on cork bark or grown in small pots of fine sphagnum with excellent drainage; intermediate temperatures and good humidity and airflow suit most collections. It produces flowers multiple times per year.

plants.nativeContext: Zootrophion is a small genus of cool-growing miniature orchids from the cloud forests of Central and South America. Known for their highly unusual trap-like flowers with small apertures and intricate internal coloring, they grow epiphytically on mossy branches in perpetually misty conditions.
plants.notes: Zootrophion sp. is a specialist cool-growing orchid for experienced collectors, requiring consistently cool temperatures below 22°C, near-saturated humidity, and excellent air movement — essentially cloud-forest conditions in a case. The bizarre flowers appear from leaf bases. It is not recommended for beginners or for tropical vivaria.

plants.nativeContext: Barbosella is a small genus of miniature cool-to-intermediate epiphytic orchids from Andean cloud forests of South America. They grow on mossy branches in high-humidity, mist-shrouded conditions, producing tiny but intricately colored flowers throughout the year.
plants.notes: Barbosella hiftzii is a true micro-orchid suited only to cool, high-humidity specialist conditions. It can be mounted on cork with live sphagnum or fine bark, with the roots kept consistently moist. The flowers are tiny but beautiful under magnification. Keep it below 22°C at all times — warmth is fatal, not just stressful. For experienced growers only.

plants.nativeContext: Bulbophyllum intersitum is a warm-to-intermediate growing epiphytic orchid from Southeast Asia's tropical forests. Like most Bulbophyllum, it grows on mossy tree bark in humid, warm rainforest conditions and produces distinctive, often unusually-shaped flowers.
plants.notes: Bulbophyllum intersitum is a warm-growing species more suited to tropical vivaria than cool-growing orchids. Mount it on cork bark and ensure the [[pseudobulb|pseudobulbs]] and roots never dry out completely between waterings; good humidity and warmth are the main requirements. The pseudobulbs store water, which provides some drought tolerance.

plants.nativeContext: Formerly placed in Cirrhopetalum, now considered Bulbophyllum farreri, this orchid originates from the Himalayan foothills through to Myanmar and southern China. It grows as an epiphyte in subtropical and warm-temperate forests and produces characteristic umbellate flower clusters with extended lateral sepals.
plants.notes: Bulbophyllum farreri is one of the more temperature-flexible Bulbophyllum relatives, tolerating intermediate to cool-warm conditions. Mount it on a bark slab and allow it to creep freely; keep it consistently moist during growth, and a slight dry rest in cooler months can encourage flowering. The umbrella-like flower clusters are its most striking feature.

plants.nativeContext: A Bulbophyllum (formerly Cirrhopetalum) species circulating in the trade as 'Taiwanese', likely originating from Taiwan's montane or low-elevation forests. Taiwan has a number of endemic or near-endemic small epiphytic orchids in this alliance.
plants.notes: Bulbophyllum sp. 'Taiwanese' follows standard warm-to-intermediate Bulbophyllum care: bark mounting, good humidity, consistent moisture, and stable temperatures. It likely comes from a mid-elevation forest habitat, which explains its intermediate temperature tolerance. It produces the characteristic umbrella-like clusters of flowers at the apex of the inflorescence.

plants.nativeContext: Haraella odorata (sometimes spelled Haerella) is a monotypic miniature orchid genus endemic to Taiwan. It grows as an epiphyte in the island's subtropical and montane forests, producing a succession of fragrant, yellow-and-purple-striped flowers from a small fan of strap-like leaves.
plants.notes: Haraella odorata is a delightful miniature orchid — compact enough for nano terrariums and surprisingly adaptable. It can be grown on bark mounts or in small baskets with fine sphagnum, and it flowers multiple times per year with a sweet fragrance. It tolerates a range of temperatures from cool subtropical to warm, though temperature extremes should be avoided.

plants.nativeContext: Peperomia angulata (syn. P. quadrangularis) is native to the tropical forests of Central and South America. It grows as a compact, trailing herb in the humid forest understorey, producing distinctive dark-green leaves with paler longitudinal stripes along the veins.
plants.notes: Peperomia angulata is a robust, low-maintenance terrarium peperomia. It performs reliably under low light. Allow the substrate to approach dryness between waterings — peperomia roots rot easily in saturated soil.

plants.nativeContext: Peperomia emarginella is a miniature creeping peperomia native to the cloud forests of the Andes, from Colombia south to Bolivia. It grows as a low herb on mossy rocks and tree bases, producing tiny notched (emarginate) leaves on fine, trailing red stems.
plants.notes: Peperomia emarginella is an ideal nano-terrarium peperomia — extremely compact and slow-growing with almost jewel-like tiny leaves that are beautiful for covering the base of hardscape. Allow the substrate to partially dry between waterings; the small leaf size means it loses moisture quickly if humidity drops, so keep it in a closed environment.

plants.nativeContext: Peperomia fagerlindii is a miniature trailing peperomia from the tropical forests of South America, characterised by small, rounded, succulent-textured leaves on delicate creeping stems.
plants.notes: Peperomia fagerlindii is a compact, well-behaved peperomia. Like all peperomia, it's semi-succulent and tolerant of occasional dry periods; the fleshy leaves store moisture and the roots are sensitive to waterlogging, so avoid overwatering.

plants.nativeContext: Peperomia guttulata is a South American peperomia with distinctive patterned leaves — typically dark green with pale silver spots or banding. It grows in the humid forest understorey, often on mossy substrate or leaf litter.
plants.notes: Peperomia guttulata is a moderately easy terrarium peperomia with attractive foliar patterning. Allow it to partially dry between waterings, and keep it in medium light to preserve leaf contrast — low light can reduce the patterning. It suits a mid-layer position in a closed tropical build.

plants.nativeContext: An informally named Peperomia form circulated in the terrarium hobby, noted for small, round leaves adorned with dark spots or speckles on a lighter background. Likely from the tropical forests of South or Central America.
plants.notes: Peperomia sp. 'Pepper Spot' is a charming, compact accent plant for terrariums. Like all peperomia, allow the substrate to partially dry between waterings and avoid overwatering. The spotted foliage makes it visually interesting even when not in flower.

plants.nativeContext: Peperomia puteolata is native to the tropical forests of Peru and surrounding South America. It produces elongated leaves with multiple parallel pale veins on dark green, the stems arranged in a whorled pattern — one of the most distinctive peperomia species in cultivation.
plants.notes: Peperomia puteolata is one of the easiest peperomia for terrariums. Its whorled, striped foliage provides good visual interest, and it tolerates moderate humidity rather than requiring saturation. Allow the substrate to partially dry between waterings; it suits both small and large enclosed builds, and periodic trimming keeps it in shape.

plants.nativeContext: Peperomia rubella is a miniature trailing peperomia native to Jamaica's montane forests. It produces tiny, oval leaves with distinctive reddish-burgundy undersides and stems, growing in a delicate trailing or hanging habit on mossy rocks and tree limbs.
plants.notes: Peperomia rubella is a charming, compact peperomia whose red-underside leaves are a feature many enthusiasts prize.  Avoid heavy watering — the small, fleshy leaves store moisture. The red coloration intensifies in brighter indirect light, and good humidity helps it stay compact and healthy.

plants.nativeContext: Peperomia serpens is a vining peperomia native to the tropical forests of Central and South America, where its long, snaking stems climb or trail through humid understorey vegetation. It produces heart-shaped, somewhat succulent leaves on wiry, flexible stems.
plants.notes: Peperomia serpens has a trailing and climbing habit that makes it an excellent choice for vertical elements in a vivarium — its stems will attach loosely to cork bark or wood. Keep it moist but allow intermittent drying to prevent root rot. It grows readily once established; trim it back if it becomes too dominant.

plants.nativeContext: Peperomia taroriana is endemic to Fiji, where it grows in the humid forested interiors of the Fijian archipelago. Island-endemic peperomia species can differ notably in leaf texture and growth form from South American relatives.
plants.notes: Peperomia taroriana is a rare Pacific island peperomia suited to warm, humid closed terrariums. Standard warm-tropical peperomia care applies: allow partial drying between waterings, provide medium humidity and warmth. Its island endemism means it's infrequently available in cultivation; source it from specialist growers.

plants.nativeContext: A Peperomia species or form originating from the Tingo María region of central Peru — a biodiverse area in the Huánuco region of the upper Amazon basin where cloud forest meets lowland jungle. Several undescribed Peperomia forms are known from this area.
plants.notes: Peperomia sp. 'Tingo Maria' comes from a transitional cloud-to-lowland zone and tolerates a useful range of humidity and temperature conditions as a result. Standard peperomia care applies: allow partial drying between waterings, avoid waterlogging, and provide warm to intermediate temperatures and moderate to high humidity in a closed build.

plants.nativeContext: Peperomia trinervis is a compact peperomia native to tropical South America and the Caribbean, growing in humid shaded forest floors and on mossy tree bases. Its three-veined leaves are characteristic of the species.
plants.notes: Peperomia trinervis is a reliable, compact terrarium peperomia whose three-veined leaf structure gives it a subtle but appealing texture. Standard peperomia care applies: moderate humidity, allow partial drying between waterings, and avoid cold and waterlogging. It suits smaller closed builds well.

plants.nativeContext: Peperomia turboensis is a small peperomia from the tropical forests of South America, notable for its compact rosette form and fleshy, patterned foliage.
plants.notes: Peperomia turboensis is a compact rosette-forming peperomia suited to closed terrariums. Its fleshy leaves store water and give it some drought tolerance; allow the substrate to partially dry between waterings. It's not commonly available, so source it from specialist vivarium plant suppliers.

plants.nativeContext: Peperomia verticillata is native to Cuba and the Caribbean, growing in rocky forest habitats. The 'Red Log' cultivar is notable for its deep crimson-red undersides contrasting with dark green upper leaf surfaces on compact whorled stems — giving a striking bicolor effect.
plants.notes: Peperomia verticillata 'Red Log' is one of the most visually striking peperomia for terrariums. The red undersides are especially impactful when the plant is viewed from the side through glass. It prefers slightly lower humidity than many tropical terrariums, which makes it a useful bridge between open and closed builds. Allow partial drying between waterings.

plants.nativeContext: Piper crocatum is a climbing pepper vine native to Peru, where it grows in the understorey of tropical and cloud-forest margins. Its leaves are dramatically patterned with pink-to-silver splotches on dark green, with deep red-purple undersides — making it one of the most ornamental foliage plants in the genus.
plants.notes: Piper crocatum is an eye-catching terrarium vine that climbs readily up branches or cork bark. The metallic leaf patterning intensifies under medium to bright indirect light; too little light and the coloration fades. Keep it consistently moist without waterlogging, trim it periodically as growth can become vigorous, and avoid cold temperatures.

plants.nativeContext: Bolbitis heudelotii is an aquatic to semi-aquatic fern native to fast-flowing tropical African rivers and streams, particularly in West and Central Africa. It grows submerged or emersed, attaching rhizomes to rocks and wood in clear, oxygenated water.
plants.notes: Bolbitis heudelotii, the African Water Fern, is best grown attached to rock or driftwood in the wet zone of a paludarium, submerged or with the rhizome partially above water. Never bury the [[rhizome]] — it must remain exposed or it will rot. The plant prefers cool, moving, oxygen-rich water when aquatic; in emersed cultivation at high humidity it produces larger, more lace-like [[frond|fronds]].

plants.nativeContext: Bolbitis heteroclita is a Southeast Asian aquatic and semi-aquatic fern growing in humid, shaded stream margins from India to the Philippines. The 'Cuspidata' form is distinguished by distinctively tufted or crested pinnae tips, making it more ornamental than the standard form.
plants.notes: Bolbitis heteroclita 'Cuspidata' can be attached by its rhizomes to wood or rock and grown submerged or at the water's edge in a paludarium. Like all Bolbitis, never bury the [[rhizome]]. The cuspidate tip form is particularly attractive in emersed cultivation, and it requires high humidity, soft to neutral water, and warm temperatures.

plants.nativeContext: Lemmaphyllum microphyllum is a miniature epiphytic fern from East and Southeast Asia, growing on mossy tree bark and rocks in humid forest environments. It produces tiny, round (coin-shaped) fronds on creeping [[rhizome|rhizomes]], carpeting surfaces with a delicate texture.
plants.notes: Lemmaphyllum microphyllum is an excellent epiphytic terrarium fern that grows flat against bark or moss walls, producing a charming mosaic of tiny round fronds. It can be attached to cork bark or grown on moist wood, and it tolerates lower temperatures than many tropical ferns. Keep it moist and humid — the small [[frond|fronds]] desiccate quickly if conditions drop.

plants.nativeContext: Pleopeltis percussa is a drought-tolerant epiphytic fern from the tropical and subtropical Americas, growing on tree bark and rocky outcrops. Its strap-shaped fronds are marked with distinctive impressed dots (punctae) on the surface, from which the common name derives.
plants.notes: Pleopeltis percussa, the Wart Fern, is a tough epiphytic fern well-suited to terrarium culture. It can survive brief dry periods by curling its [[frond|fronds]] in a manner similar to resurrection ferns, and it rewets readily from dormancy. It can be mounted on bark or grown in a well-draining mix, and it's easier to care for than many of the more delicate terrarium ferns.

plants.nativeContext: Pyrrosia nummularifolia is a miniature creeping epiphytic fern from Southeast Asia (Thailand, Malaysia, Indonesia), producing tiny round, coin-shaped fronds on creeping rhizomes. It grows on mossy bark in humid tropical and subtropical forest.
plants.notes: Pyrrosia nummularifolia is a popular terrarium fern for its miniature, perfectly round [[frond|fronds]] that carpet bark and wood surfaces over time. Keep the [[rhizome|rhizomes]] exposed rather than buried, attach it to cork or a wood panel, and provide moderate to high humidity and stable warmth for the best growth. It's very slow-growing, but it forms attractive dense mats once established.

plants.nativeContext: Tectaria zeilanica is a tropical fern native to Sri Lanka and South Asia, growing in deeply shaded, humid forest floors where moisture is consistently high. The fronds are large, divided, and have an almost lace-like quality, making it visually impressive.
plants.notes: Tectaria zeilanica, the Ceylon Tectaria, is suited to large, mature closed terrariums or Wardian cases where consistent deep shade and high humidity can be maintained. The [[frond|fronds]] are delicate and do not tolerate direct light or dry spells; plant it in deep, rich, moist substrate. It's a striking architectural fern once established.

plants.nativeContext: Actiniopteris australis is a distinctive fern from arid and semi-arid rocky habitats across Africa, the Middle East, and South Asia. Its fan-shaped, palm-like [[frond|fronds]] are highly adapted to drought, allowing the plant to survive in rock crevices and dry hillsides where most ferns cannot.
plants.notes: Actiniopteris australis, the Palm Fern, is an unusual arid-adapted fern completely unlike typical terrarium ferns. It's best suited to a dryland or desert open terrarium rather than a closed humid build — allow the substrate to dry thoroughly between waterings and provide bright, indirect to partial direct light. It's very drought-tolerant once established but dislikes overwatering.

plants.nativeContext: Hatiora salicornioides (dancing bones or drunkard's dream) is an epiphytic cactus native to the humid tropical Atlantic Forest of eastern Brazil. Unlike desert cacti it grows on trees in warm, humid conditions, producing yellow-orange flowers at the tips of small segmented stems.
plants.notes: Hatiora salicornioides, the Dancing Bones Cactus, is a forest epiphyte that prefers higher humidity and more water than desert cacti — but still needs good drainage and periodic drying between waterings. It can be mounted on bark or grown in a fast-draining bark-perlite mix; bright indirect light and moderate humidity in an open terrarium suit it well. A brief cooler, drier rest period in winter will encourage spring blooming.

plants.nativeContext: Muehlenbeckia complexa is native to New Zealand and parts of Australia where it forms dense, scrambling tangles of wiry stems across coastal scrub, dunes, and rocky hillsides. Its tiny round leaves on hair-fine, dark stems create an almost moss-like visual effect at scale.
plants.notes: Muehlenbeckia complexa, the Wire Vine, is one of the easiest trailing vines for open terrariums and desert-style builds. It grows vigorously once established and needs regular trimming to prevent it from dominating an enclosure. It prefers cooler temperatures and lower humidity than tropical plants, making it ideal for temperate or Mediterranean-style terrariums.

plants.nativeContext: A variegated cultivar of the New Zealand wire vine, with irregular cream, white, or pale-green splashing on the tiny round leaves. The wiry stems remain dark, creating a striking contrast with the light variegation.
plants.notes: Muehlenbeckia complexa 'Variegated' grows more slowly than the standard green form because of its reduced chlorophyll, and it needs more light than the green form to maintain the variegation and grow well. The same cool, open terrarium conditions as the species apply: good airflow, allow partial drying, and cool temperatures. Trim it regularly to keep it compact.

plants.nativeContext: Selaginella erythropus 'Sanguinea' is a form of the red spike moss native to the humid tropical forests of Central and South America. The 'Sanguinea' selection is chosen for its especially vivid blood-red to ruby coloration on the upper [[frond]] surface, contrasting with pale green undersides.
plants.notes: Selaginella erythropus 'Sanguinea' produces its red coloration from anthocyanin pigments, and the color is most vivid under low to medium indirect light at high humidity — counter-intuitively, bright light can actually reduce the color intensity. It can be grown in consistently moist fine peat or coco-coir and makes excellent ground cover for tropical closed builds. High ambient humidity is required.

plants.nativeContext: Selaginella martensii 'Watsoniana' is a cultivar of a Mexican and Central American spike moss species, selected for variegated silver-white tips on the frond ends that give a frosted or snow-dusted appearance to the otherwise green foliage.
plants.notes: Selaginella martensii 'Watsoniana' is an extremely attractive selaginella for closed builds — the silver-tipped [[frond|fronds]] glow under terrarium lighting. It prefers slightly cooler temperatures and lower humidity than strictly tropical species, making it more versatile than many selaginellas. Keep it moist at all times; it's a reliable and forgiving ground cover.

plants.nativeContext: Solanum evolvulifolium is a miniature trailing nightshade native to Central and South America, where it grows as a small vining herb in humid forest margins and scrub. Unlike typical large nightshades, it produces tiny bindweed-like leaves on delicate twining stems.
plants.notes: Solanum evolvulifolium is an unusual and uncommon terrarium plant grown for its fine, delicate trailing habit rather than large foliage. Its miniature leaves and wiry stems create an intricate texture in the mid to background. Keep it moist with moderate humidity, and note that all parts of Solanum plants may be toxic if ingested.

plants.nativeContext: Solanum uleanum is a miniature nightshade from the tropical rainforests of Brazil and adjacent South America, described by the botanist Ernst Ule. It is a compact, soft-stemmed trailing species occasionally grown in specialist vivarium collections for its delicate foliage.
plants.notes: Solanum uleanum is a specialist vivarium plant rarely seen in general cultivation. It can be grown as a trailing vine in a warm, humid, closed terrarium with moist substrate and indirect light. Like all Solanum, all parts should be considered potentially toxic. Source it from specialist vivarium plant dealers.

plants.nativeContext: Pilea libanensis 'Aquamarine' is a cultivar of a small Pilea species producing a cloud of delicate, thread-like stems and tiny round leaves with a blue-green to aquamarine tint. It grows as a groundcover in humid subtropical to tropical forests.
plants.notes: Pilea libanensis 'Aquamarine' creates a stunning, airy effect in terrariums — the fine stems and tiny leaves resemble a miniature cloud or soft moss from a distance. It prefers moderate to high humidity and moist but well-drained substrate. Trim it periodically to keep it dense and compact.

plants.nativeContext: Pilea pubescens 'Silver Cloud' is a Caribbean and tropical South American Pilea cultivar selected for heavily silver-frosted foliage — the metallic sheen covering most of the leaf surface gives the impression of a silver cloud above the green base.
plants.notes: Pilea pubescens 'Silver Cloud' is a striking metallic-leaved pilea well-suited to closed terrariums. The silver coloration is brightest under medium light — too low and it fades, too high and it bleaches. Keep it moist and humid; it's compact and relatively well-behaved.

plants.nativeContext: Pilea spruceana is native to the cool cloud forests of the Andes in Peru, Ecuador, and Bolivia. It grows in intermediate-temperature humid forest understorey, producing deeply textured, chocolate-brown leaves with a contrasting silver metallic sheen running between the raised veins.
plants.notes: Pilea spruceana is one of the most ornamental Pilea species for terrariums, with dramatic texture and silver-on-brown coloration that are most vivid in medium indirect light. It prefers cooler temperatures than typical tropicals — 16–24°C is ideal — and the substrate should be allowed to approach partial dryness between waterings to avoid root saturation.

plants.nativeContext: Procris pulchra is a small trailing herb in the nettle family (Urticaceae) native to humid tropical forests of Southeast Asia, where it grows on moist rocks, mossy soil, and stream banks in deeply shaded conditions. The leaves are marked with distinctive silver or pale patterning on a dark green base.
plants.notes: Procris pulchra is a rarely cultivated but beautiful terrarium plant with fittonia-like silver patterning on dark leaves. It requires consistently high humidity and shade — similar conditions to fittonia but even more shade-tolerant — and the substrate should be kept moist at all times. It's a rewarding accent plant for mature, stable closed terrariums.

plants.nativeContext: Cissus amazonica is a climbing vine native to the rainforests of the Amazon basin in Brazil and neighboring South America. It produces lanceolate, dark-green leaves marked with a distinctive silver chevron pattern along the [[midrib]] and lateral veins, with deep red-purple undersides.
plants.notes: Cissus amazonica is an excellent large-build terrarium vine with striking metallic foliage that climbs readily on wood, cork, or background panels. Keep it consistently moist with high humidity — the large leaf area transpires rapidly in dry conditions. Vigorous growth means regular trimming may be needed in enclosed spaces; it's best suited to large vivaria or Wardian cases.

plants.nativeContext: Cissus discolor is native to the tropical forests of Southeast Asia (Java, Borneo, Cambodia), where it grows as a vigorous climbing vine in humid forest edges and clearings. Its large leaves display spectacular red, silver, and green iridescent patterning with deep purple undersides — resembling rex begonias, hence the common name.
plants.notes: Cissus discolor, the Rex Begonia Vine, is one of the most dramatically patterned climbing plants available for terrariums. It's best suited to a large, open-top Wardian case or large vivarium where its vigorous growth can be accommodated. Bright indirect light is needed to maintain the spectacular leaf coloration; keep it moist and trim it regularly, as it will climb aggressively up any support.
